meaning: possessive suffix pronoun of the 2nd masc. sg.; subject suffix pronoun of the 2nd masc. sg. of the verb in the perfect and the gerund; object suffix pronoun of the 2nd masc. singular.
possessive suffix pronoun of the 2nd fem. sg.; subject suffix pronoun of the 2nd fem. sg. of the verb in the perfect and the gerund; object suffix pronoun of the 2nd fem. singular.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: for more details, see Brockelmann 1908:309-310; Barth 1913:43-48. ETHIOPIC: for more details, see Leslau 1956:58.

meaning: 'now, then, so then, thus, therefore, of course, indeed, accordingly, to be sure, in fact, and yet, even, as for'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Heb. kī 'surely, yea, then' (see V. Christian, WZKM 31[1924].178-182; Baumgartner 43), Ug.Ph. k, Aram.Akk. kī. See also D. Cohen 1970:16, under yy. ETHIOPIC: Tna. -kā, kā'a 'then', -kā 'and, then', Gur. xeta (from xe-ta) 'so then', Chaha -xe 'to be sure'. For kā in combination with various elements, see Dillmann 1907:120.

meaning: an element expressing distance, as in zakku, ʔantakku, ʔallənku
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. hunā-ka 'there', Heb. kākā 'thus', Aram. de-k 'that'. For more details, see Barth 1913:80-3.

meaning: 'dig'; probably a misreading for kʷaʔata (see kʷaʔata 'dig').
'measure for liquids, clay vessel, jar for transport and storage of water'
origin_explanation: from Gk. khoeis (pl. of khoeus) 'measure for liquids'.
ETHIOPIC: Te. kʷaʔaba 'to double', Anc. Har. kʷaʔab 'second', mod. Har. kāb 'additional (food)', Amh. kʷaʔəb 'second order of the Ethiopian syllabary'

meaning: 'second, other, companion' (for the meanings 'other, companion', cp. also kālaʔ);
'secondly, another time, a second time, twice, two (followed by a measure), again once more, furthermore, besides, moreover'
'doubly, twofold, twofold more, repeatedly, twice, double portion, -fold (with any number)'
'doubling, double (n), double amount, two parts, twofold, multiplied, -fold (with any number)'
'twofold, doubly, a second time, twice, -fold (with any number)' (bəzuha makʷʔabita 'manifold'); (ʔa)kʷaʔbata, see below (ʔa)makʷʔaba, (ta)makʷʔaba, (ʔa)makʷʔabata; see below

(from G. kʷəbā) kʷəbā ‘do twice (the work), make twice (the number)’. Gur. kʷəbā ‘do something again’. Tna. kʷəbī ‘measure of cereals equivalent to about two quintals’. For kʷəb ‘doubly twined’, cp. Amh. kʷəb ‘spool of thread’.
origin_explanation: (from G. kʷəbā) kʷəbā ‘do twice (the work), make twice (the number)’. Gur. kʷəbā ‘do something again’. Tna. kʷəbī ‘measure of cereals equivalent to about two quintals’. For kʷəb ‘doubly twined’, cp. Amh. kʷəb ‘spool of thread’.
ETHIOPIC: perhaps Amh. kʷəbā ‘build a dry wall of stones or bricks’, that is, without cement.

ETHIOPIC: denominative from a form such as kʷə(a)-bat: Te. kʷəbāt ‘loan at hundred percent interest’ (that is, double), Tna. akʷəbātä ‘double (v)’, Amh. (tä)kʷəbātä ‘double’ (intr); passed into Cushitic: Sa. kʷəbat ‘replacement at hundred percent’, Bil. kʷəbāt.
ETHIOPIC: Te. kʷəʾən ‘truffle’, Tna. kʷəʾənti, Amh. kʷəʾənti, kunti ‘a vegetable slightly larger than a lentil which is eaten only during famine’.

meaning: ‘excavating (n), hole’; from kʷəʾəta I ‘dig’ (below), with augmented n.
‘excrement, dung, (T.M.Sw. in D 866) contents of the stomach’;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to Ar. ġars ‘excrement’ (Dillmann 866).

ETHIOPIC: Te. kʷərsä ‘broken meat in an animal’s stomach’, Te. kʷərsē, kʷərasē ‘animal’s stomach’, Tna. kʷərsī, Amh. kʷərs ‘kus ‘excrement (usually of fowl)’, also in Cushitic: Bil. kʷəʾad (pl. kʷəʾas) ‘stomach, contents of the stomach, excrement’.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. kʷəʾəso, Amh.Gur. kʷəʾas, Arg. kʷəʾasä, Har. in atännäkʷəš ‘ball’; also in Cushitic ʾic: Sa. kʷəʾso.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. kʷəʾätä ‘dig, break up the soil’, Amh. kʷəʾatä ‘break up the soil’, kʷəʾätäkʷä ‘cultivate, weed’. See also kʷəʾasa, and kʷəʾənt III.
Dillmann 1399 suggests the reading ‘agāta’.

meaning: ‘dung, manure, cow dung that serves as fuel’.

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. kʷəʾawä ‘spill, pour out’, Te. kʷəʾa, Har. (a)kʷəʾa, Gur. kʷəʾä.

from Amh. kʷəb ‘fort’; possibly of Cushitic (Or. kʷəb) origin; also in Gur. kʷəb ‘wall made of stone’, Gaf. kʷəbä ‘village’. W. Müller, ZDMG 131 (1981). 400 compares, with reservations, SAr. kʷəb-t ‘part of dam structure’, and perhaps Ar. (Yem) kʷəbä ‘pillar’ (Müller).
ETHIOPIC: Har. kʷəbī ‘calf of leg’ (quoted in Reinisch 1902:236); also Som. kʷəb.

SEMITIC: Ar. kʷəba ‘sweepings’, Ar. (Dat) kʷəbī ‘dry dung’, Aram. kʷəbūyā, Syr. kʷəbūṭā, Akk. kʷəbī ‘excrement, dung’. For the biradical root, see Noeldeke 1910:162–3, contrary to Levy II, p. 284 who derives the Aram.-Syr. noun from kʷəb ‘be round’. ETHIOPIC: Te. kʷəbo, kʷəboṭāt ‘dry excrement of animal’, Tna. kʷəbo, Amh.Arg.Gaf. kʷəbāt, Gur. kʷəbä, also in Cushitic: Had kʷəbäṭa, Aw. kʷəbʾet, Or kʷəbota.

meaning: ‘encircle, surround, go around in a circle, (K*) gather, (Lt) roll or into a ball’;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. kʷəbaba ‘form into a ball’, kʷəbba ‘ball of thread, lump’, kʷəbāb ‘ball of thread’ (Dizy), PBH. kʷirkeb ‘encircle’ (from kʷirkeb), by dissimilation; see Rūžička 119). ETHIOPIC: Tna. kʷəbābä ‘surround’, Te. kʷəbba (kʷəb), Amh. kʷəbābä, (an)kʷəbābä ‘make something round like a ball’ (from kʷəb-kʷəb), Gur. kʷəb ‘surround’, passed into Cushitic: Qab. kʷəbabbo, Had. kʷəbbä-kʷəbbo, Bil. kʷəbāb ‘go around, surround’. See also kʷəbaba II.

meaning: ‘appoint to an office, invest with an office’;
origin_explanation: denominative from makʷəb (see kʷəbaba I).

meaning: ‘be heavy, be burden, be serious, be severe (sickness) come hardened (heart)’; with indirect object suffix pronouns ‘be heavy for’;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. kʷəbada ‘afflict, press hard’, takʷəbada ‘thicken (milk)’, SAr. (Min) kʷəbdt ‘taxes’, Heb. kʷəbēd ‘be heavy’, Ug. kʷbd ‘heavy’, Akk. kʷəbātu, kʷəbādu ‘be heavy’; developed into ‘honor, glory’: Heb. kʷəbōd, Ph.Ug. kʷbd, Akk. kʷəbātu ‘become honored’. ETHIOPIC: Te. kʷəbda ‘be heavy’, Tna. kʷəbādä, Amh.Gur. kʷəbādä, Arg. kʷəbādä, Gur. (a)kʷəbādä ‘respect, honor’. See also kʷəbd.

SEMITIC: from the root kʷbd ‘be heavy’ (for ‘liver’, originally ‘heavy heart’, see Buck 252); Ar. kʷəbid ‘liver’, Soq. šibdeh (with ki > š), Heb. kʷəbēd, Aram.-Syr. kʷəbda, Md. kʷəbda, Ug. kʷbd, Akk. kʷəbattu, kʷəbittu. ETHIOPIC: Te. kʷəbəd ‘belly’, Tna. kʷəbdī, Gur. kʷəbd, Amh. hōd (< kʷəbd > *hōd > *hōd > hod), Har. kʷəd (with b > *b > *w > zero).
perhaps a misprint or a misreading for kʷəbata hōdī.

meaning: ‘give a wedding feast, cause to celebrate a wedding’;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Noeldeke 1904:112, n. 3 connects it with Ar. wakʷəba, but none of the meanings of Ar. wakʷəba fits the meaning of kʷəbkʷəba. Bittner, WZKM 27 (1913) 129 compares Soq. ʾekʷəb ‘enter’ (add Mh. wēkʷəb), with the specific meaning ‘enter the bridegroom’s house’; for the development of the meanings, cp. Amh. agʷəbba ‘marry’, lit. ‘bring (into the house)’.

meaning: 'hold a feast or wedding reception'; connected by Dillmann 1432 with Te. kəbkäba 'hop, skip', that is 'dance', the dancing being an essential part of the wedding festivities. For kəbkäb 'assembly, congregation', cp. Te. kəbkäba 'gather'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. kəbkäba 'give a banquet', Amh. kəbkäba 'hold a feast or wedding reception'; connected by Dillmann 1432 with Te. kəbkäba 'hop, skip', that is 'dance', the dancing being an essential part of the wedding festivities. For kəbkäb 'assembly, congregation', cp. Te. kəbkäba 'gather'.

meaning: 'hook, ring (on a sandal or on a carrying pole), loop'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. kabl, kibl 'leg iron, chain, shackle' (for its Syriac origin, see Fraenkel 243), Heb. kebel 'fetter, bond' (Aramaism according to Wagner 64), Aram. kiblā, Syr. keblā, Md. kublia 'bonds, fetters', Akk. kabālu 'tie, fetter' (Zimmern 35); also in Cushitic: Sa. kəbälo 'traveling bag'. See M. Cohen 1926 7:103, and kənbäl, below.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. kʷəblalä 'stroll about', Te. (tə)kəbläla 'roll while falling', Amh. kʷəbälälä 'run away'.

SEMITIC: Ar. kəbaro considered by Noeldeke 1910:56 a Geez loanword, Ar. (Sudan) kəbūr 'drum'. ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna.Amh. kəbaro 'drum', Arg. kəräbo, Har. kəräbu, Amh.Gaf.Gur. kəbäro, kəräbo; also in Cushitic: Sa.Af. kəbaro, Bil. kəbūr, Aw. kiriwi.

meaning: 'woven basket'; metathesis in relation to kəräbo.

'be honored, be glorious, be magnificent, be great, be famous, be illustrious, be precious'; 'akbara, causative; also 'pay honor, glorify, venerate, value'; takəbara 'be honored, behave haughtily, boast'; takəbara 'honor one another'; kəbur 'honored, honorable, glorious, magnificent, famous, illustrious, noble, precious'; kəburat (Gr) 'exploits'; kəbr 'honor, glory, reverence, prestige, splendor, pomp, magnificence'.

'name of a type of a Geez poem, lit. "she is the glory"'; (kəbra nagaśt 'The Glory of the King's', title of a book "which has as its central-piece the legend of the Queen of Sheba" [see Ullendorff 1968:74ff., 139ff; Hammerschmidt, in Assfalg-Krüger 164).
'honor, (Gr) glorification'; 'akbäri, part. of 'akbara; 'akbärot 'honor'; takäbäri, part. of takäbara; takäbä(a)ro (Gr.464) 'honor, esteem'.
'be big, be great, be SAT. kəbr 'great, magistrate', Heb. kəbīr 'powerful', Aram.-Syr. kəbar 'abound, increase', Md. kəbar 'great', Akk. kəbāru 'become fat, thick,
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. kəbura 'be big, be great, be SAT. kəbr 'great, magistrate', Heb. kəbīr 'powerful', Aram.-Syr. kəbar 'abound, increase', Md. kəbar 'great', Akk. kəbāru 'become fat, thick, ETHIOPIC: Tna. kəbära 'be honored', Amh.Gur. kəbbära; Gur. kəbbära 'become nominative from Ar. kəbīr 'rich'; also a kəbbärte 'rich', Arg. kəbar; passed into Bil.Sa kəbar 'be honored', Be. kəbär.

From Ar. kibrīt 'sulphur' (for its Syriac origin, see Fraenkel 153; considered by Zimmern 60 to be of Akkadian [kibritu] origin); also in Te. kərbīt 'wood for making fire', Tna. kərbīt 'matches', Amh. kəbrīt.

meaning: 'wrap (a cloth or scarf around the head), wind around, decorate (the hair), fasten with a hairnet, tie up (the hair with cloth)'; takabsa, passive; kəbso 'hairnet, woman's covering, chaplet worn around the head, head veil cloth'; kəbasa (M) 'headband, diadem, (Gr.263) bracelet worn by women, ornament of women'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. kəbasa 'surround (a town), lay siege', kəbasa raʔsahu fī 'muffle one's head (with a garment)'. ETHIOPIC: Te. kəbsa 'fold', Amh. kəbbäsa 'wrap cloth around one's head', kəbbäsa 'wrapping-cloth around one's head'.
Dillmann 1432 interprets it as a transcription of Heb. kāpīs 'stuccowork (laid over paneling)'. Note that the Asmara Bible does not have this noun for 1 Kings 7:2 (= 1 Kings 7:2) quoted by Dillmann.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. kəbbäsä 'make the leg thick', kʷəbäs 'sore which does not heal, elephantiasis'.

meaning: 'hide, conceal, keep secret, take away by hiding'; takabta, passive; also 'disappear'; kəbäti, act. part. of kəbata; kəbut, pass. part. of kəbata; also 'secret, clandestine'; kəbut-a, ba-kəbut 'secretly'; kəbäta 'hidden thing, hidden nature, secret'; kəbtat 'act of hiding, concealing, keeping secret'; kəbutäwi 'hidden, concealed, secret, pertaining to hidden things'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. kəbata in kəbata ḡayzahu fī ḡawfihi rendered by Lane 2583 'he restrained his rage in his insides (or bosom)', but by Hava 640 'he concealed his anger in his bosom'. Bittner, WZKM 30 (1917-18). 425 identifies it with Soq. qabit 'teach', and the meanings 'teach, hide' he compares Ar. 'allama 'teach', and Heb. 'lm (nif-al) 'hide'. This identification is not convincing. ETHIOPIC: Tna. kəbätä 'hide', Te. kəbt 'treasure', Amh. kəbbätä 'steal money and go into hiding'.
SEMITIC: Ar. kəbata 'debase'.
is probably a transcription of Heb. kābā (kby) 'be extinguished'.
corruption of Gk. koinobion 'monastery'; see also kənubyā, below.
from Ar. ḫabaza 'bake', ḫubz 'bread' (with ḫ be coming k in the loanwords); also in Som. kibis 'bread'. For the Geez root, see ḫabaza, above.

meaning: 'betray, abandon'; 'akdʔa (DTW), causative; takadʔa (DTW), passive; takadʔa (DTW) 'betray one another'; kadʔi (DTW), act. part. of kədʔa; kadʔot (DTW) 'betrayal'.
origin_explanation: from Ar. ḫadāʔa; also Te. kədʔa 'betray', Tna. kədʔe, Amh.Gur. kədäda, Har. xädäʔa.
variant of karakand.

meaning: 'bind, tie'; MA has kodkoda with the Amharic translation näqqäfä 'blame'. It is quite possible that näqqäfä is a misprint or a misreading for əqqäfä 'embrace, take in one's arms', a meaning that comes close to 'bind, tie' given by H;
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. kʷəkkʷədä (< kʷədʔädä) 'tie', Amh. kʷədäkkʷədä 'tie up (a criminal or a prisoner)'.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. kʷədäkkʷədä, same.

meaning: 'cover, wrap, clothe, hide, veil, close, protect, forgive (sins)'; 'akdäna, causative; takdäna, takadäna 'be covered, cover oneself, be clothed, clothe oneself, be protected'; kədäni, act. part. of kədäna; also 'protector'; kədun 'covered, hidden, protected'; kədän 'cover, covering, hiding place, garment, tunic, veil placed on top of the ṣəwwa and säḥn (for which see below); kədnät 'covering, protection'; makdən, makdən, makdənt, (T) makdän, (T) makdäni (pl. makdänəm, makdämt, makdäntät) 'cover, covering, veil, silk veil of an altar'; (makdäna rəʔs 'helmet'); makdän 'cover'.
cover > thatch a house, put on clothes, protect
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: 'cover > thatch a house, put on clothes, protect': Ar. kadana bi 'wrap oneself in (clothes)', Ar. (Dat) makdana 'cover of an oven', Akk. kadānu 'protect'. Brockelmann 1928:318 also compares Aram.-Syr. kədan 'yoke, couple, bind' (cp. also Md. kədn). Praetorius 1890:25 derives G. kdn from Ar. ik-t-anna 'be covered'; Brockelmann 1908:533, n. 3 rightly expresses doubts about this comparison. ETHIOPIC: Tna. kādānā 'cover, clothe', Te. kādāna 'cover', Amh. kādādānā 'cover the roof with straw, cover a pot', Har. xādāna 'cover, thatch', Gur. kādānā 'cover with a lid', tāxādādārā (with r < n) 'wear clothes'; passed into Cushitic: Bil. kadān 'cover'.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. kādāwā 'brocade' (from Geez).

meaning: heel, palm of hand, sole of foot'
origin_explanation: probably transcription of Heb. kap 'palm of hand'; also Amh. kaf (from Geez). In the other Semitic languages: Ar. kaff 'palm of hand', Mh. keff 'back of hand', Aram-Syr. kappā 'palm of hand', Md. kapa, Ug. kp, Akk. kappu.
is either an extension of kāf I 'sole of foot', or confused with kuf (for which see below).

meaning: stone', nickname of the apostle Peter;
origin_explanation: from Aram.-Syr. kepā 'rock, stone'; Heb. kep comes likewise from Aramaic (Wagner 65). Akk. kāpu 'cliff'; for kefās, cp. Gk. kēphās; ETHIOPIC: Amh. kefā 'stone, precious stone' (from Geez).
from Ar. huff; also in Amh. kuf, kof, Har. xuffi. See also kāf II.

meaning: become blunt, become dull (eye), become obtuse, become weak', 'akfāfa 'causative', takafā 'become blunt'

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. kāf'e 'be bad', Te. kāf'a, Amh.Arg.Gur. kafu 'bad, evil'.

meaning: distribute, divide, separate, distinguish, allot, assign, apportion, give a share, grant, bestow, impart, make someone a participant'; takafala, passive; also 'be given a portion' (za'iyyətkāffal 'indivisible'); takāfala 'share among themselves, divide up among themselves, become a partner, participate'; 'astakāfala, caus. of takāfala; also 'divide among many'; kafl, act. part. of kafala; kaful, pass. part. of kafala; kafl 'distribution'; kafl 'part, portion, share, lot, division, tion, category, chapter, section, verse'; kufāle (k°āfāle) 'partition, division, tion, separation'; (masāhafa kufāle 'Book of Jubilees'); bibliography, see Hammerschmidt-Six 1983:278; kaflāt 'division, distribution, (Lt) portion'; makfāl (Gr) 'cross-roads'; makfalt 'part, portion, share, lot, division, tafalot (Lt) 'share';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. kifl 'part, share', kafala 'provide for someone', Mh. kafelēt 'half of a fraction'. For its relation with Heb. kāpal 'fold, double', see Dillmann 876, Noeldeke 1910:97; also Ug. kpl 'duality'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. kāfəlā 'pay, divide, share'; Amh. kāffālā, Arg. kāfālā, Gur. kāfālā; passed into Cushitic: Sid. kaffali, Dar. kafale, Or. kafala.

SEMITIC: Ar. kafana 'envelop, shroud', Sh. əkəfən 'enshroud'. ETHIOPIC: Te. kāfəna 'wrap in shrouds', Har. kāfəna, Gur. gāfənā; Te. kāfən 'shroud', Amh.Har.Gur. gāfən, Gaf. kāfənā; passed into Cushitic: Or. kafana, Sa. kafan.

SEMITIC: Ar. kāfira 'jar' (from kafara 'cover, conceal'), Heb. kəpōr 'bowl, jar', Syr. kapurṭā, Akk. kapru, a type of sacrifice and the platter for it. ETHIOPIC: Tna. kāfər 'big basket'; also in Cushitic: Bil. kabār (pl. kafāt). See also M. Cohen 1926-7:95.
from Ar. kafar 'the netherworld'.
it is possible that Amh. affārā has here the meaning 'turn to dust', and G. kafara would then be a denominative of kafar II.
from Sanskrit karpūra through Ar. kāfūr (Littmann 1926:415); also Amh. kanfur. For Ar. kāfūr, see Jeffery 246.

meaning: kind of vestment of a bishop, (T.M) headgear of a bishop';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. kafara 'cover, hide', kāfiru d-durū'i 'garment that is worn over a coat of mail'. For Heb. kipper 'make good, pardon sin, make atonement' for (also Ar. kaffāra 'atonement') from an original meaning 'cover', see Koehler 452, Baumgartner 470. ETHIOPIC: Amh. kufar 'silken turban, white silk cloth'.
from Gk. kai phaz 'and phaz' (transcription of Heb. ūpāz) read kaiphas as one word (Euringer, OC 24[1927]:145). See also qəfāz.

meaning: form a group or a party'; makfat (K*) 'clan, tribe'.
Amh. abāččā 'separate, divide; make fun of, belittle'.

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. kāfāy 'kind of silken shirt', Amh.

meaning: kind of fine cloth', Har. kāfəyi 'kind of cloth'. See also kafāyā II.

origin_explanation: from Ar. kafā (kfy) 'be sufficient'; also in Te. kāfā.

meaning: see kafāwī. makfe 'wrapper, cover, belt, (Lt) towel, (L) bundle, parcel, (KG) wraparound, apron-like garment'.

origin_explanation: see also 'akāhī.

meaning: (imperf. yəxl, subj. yaxhal) 'be able, can do things, prevail (against), overcome, master, have power to do things, avail (against)'; with object suffixes: 'be able to endure, be possible for one' (i-kəhla 'be unable, be inept'); (za-yaxl 'able'); 'akhalā 'enable, make able, make prevail, give superiority, give strength'; takahlā 'be possible (also used with object suffixes), gain power over'; takāhalā 'agree (with), come to an agreement (with), be in accord (with)', lit. 'make things be possible for one another'; 'astakāhala (K*) 'make agree'; kahalī 'mighty, all-powerful, capable'; (kahāle k°allu 'omnipotent'); kahul (Gr.255) 'powerful'; kəhəl, kəhl, kəhəlot 'means, ability, power, faculty'; 'akhālī, makhāl (K*), part. of 'akhalā; takahālī 'possible'; takahəlo 'power'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. (Dat) kahil 'be able', kāhil 'powerful', Ar. kahala 'reach maturity', that is, 'be in a position to do things', SAr. khl 'be successful', Soq. kḥel 'be able, know', Aram. kḥel 'be able'; related to Heb. yākōl 'be able'. ETHIOPIC: Te. kāhala 'be able', Tna. kāwəlā, Amh.Gur. čala, Arg. čahala, Gaf. šala 'know', Gur. xarā 'know'. The palatalized č, š comes from a form such as *yakhal > *yakayal > yačal, yašal. The root passed into Cushitic: Bil. kahal 'be able'.

meaning: 'plaything that one places on the forehead on the Thursday after the fast of Nineve to amuse people'.
'refuse, dirt, rubbish, sweepings, filth, scum, dregs, mud, things without value, trifle, little thing';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. qaḫila 'be unclean' (with the variant k:q). Dillmann 813 compares Ar. qaḫila 'be unclean' (with the variant k:q).
SEMITIC: Ar. kuḫaylā 'marigold' (Dillmann 1856: 239).

meaning: 'ball of wood or woven leather, game in which spear-like sticks are thrown at a target (often a cactus or euphorbia), an object thrown (to serve) as a target';
origin_explanation: note that T.K. and Sw. in D 813 also have the Amharic translation alama 'target' which may come from the above-mentioned practice of throwing at a target in the kʷäḫälä-game.
probably the same as kʷäḫälä V.

meaning: 'fox-like animal, (K*) solitary animal of the wilderness';
origin_explanation: see kʷäḫälä IV.
ETHIOPIC: perhaps Amh. (ma)kaḫal 'container of skin'.

meaning: hʷU 'priest, clergyman'; (kʷäḫan 'abiyy 'high priest'); (kʷäḫan näsfq, näsfqa kʷäḫan 'assistant priest'); (liqa kʷäḫnāt 'high priest'); kʷäḫnāt 'priests, clergy'; takäḫna 'perform the priestly duties, officiate as priest, be assigned the office of priesthood' (denominative); kʷäḫnāt 'priesthood, office of priesthood, sacerdotal duty'; (liqannā kʷäḫnāt 'high priesthood'); (za-kʷäḫnāt 'priestly, sacerdotal)';
'sacerdotal, priestly'; takäḫno 'ministration of a priest, priesthood, office of a priest';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. kāhin 'diviner, priest', Heb. kohen 'priest', Aram.-Syr. kāhnā, Md. kahna, Ph.Ug. kḫn. On the Aramaic origin of G. kāhon, and Ar. kāhin, see Noeldeke 1910:36, and n. 6, Jeffery 247. On the etymology of kāhin, see bibliography in Baumgartner 440. See also Rabin 1976:246. ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh. kahan 'priest'.
probably misprint or misreading of kʷarapa; see korep II.

phonetics: kʷäḫa, kʷäḫa hʷḫa i hʷḫa, (K) kʷäḫā hʷ
meaning: 'yonder, away, to yonder place, thither, farther on, down there'; bakäḫa 'in yonder place, thither, on the other side, beyond'; ḫaba kʷäḫa 'to yonder'; ʾəmkʷäḫa 'from yonder, from the other side'; kʷäḫaka, kʷäḫak, kʷäḫ(ə)ka 'there, back there, yonder, on that side, onwards, beyond, (and) upwards'; bakäḫaka 'there, beyond'; ʾəmkʷäḫaka, ʾəmkʷäḫak 'on that side'; with suffixes, as in kʷäḫakātīhomu 'beyond them';
origin_explanation: kʷäḫa is composed of ka and ḫa; kʷäḫak is composed of ka-ḫa-k. The initial k is to be identified with the preposition kʷa 'from, to' (Amh.Gaf.), ke (Mh.Soq), and probably Tna. kʷaḫ 'from' from ka and -ab. The final k is an element expressing distance; cp. Ar. dā 'that', Aram.-kā, Heb. la-kā 'there' (in lek ləkā 'go thither'). See also Dillmann 1907:116, 377; Brockelmann 1908:323; Rundgren 1950:105, 117.

phonetics: kʷäḫā I, kʷäḫā hʷḫā i hʷḫā, (T*) kʷäḫā
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. kʷaya 'weeping willow', Tna. kʷiḫa.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. kʷaḫālä 'crackle', Amh. kʷa alā; also in Cushitic: Sa.Af. kʷāh 'sound, resound'.

meaning: kʷäḫda hʷU 'deny, abjure, denounce, repudiate, renounce, rebel, be perfidious, be treacherous, disobedient, turn aside, apostatize'; ʾakʷḫada 'convict of falsehood, contradict, faith, be an unbeliever, not believe, lead from faith, lead to apostasy'; also causative of kʷäḫda; takʷäḫda 'pass. of kʷäḫda; takʷäḫadä 'argue with, dispute, contradict one another, disagree with one another, oppose, stimulate controversy, be an adversary, contest'; ʾastakʷḫada, ʾastakʷḫadä 'induce to fall away from a faith, create controversy, declare untrue, deny'; kʷäḫādī 'infidel, impious, unbeliever, rebel, rebellious, apostate, disobedient, contentious'; kʷäḫud (Gr.257) 'infidel, apostate'; kʷäḫd 'lack of faith, apostasy, impiety, perfidy, disobedience, denial, rebellion, sedition, contention, controversy, contradiction, offense, stumbling'; kʷäḫid, makʷḫad, makʷḫadi, part. of kʷäḫada; takʷäḫ(ə)do 'argument, strife, dispute';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. (Dat) kʷhd 'deny', Heb. kʷhd (piʿel) 'hide', 'conceal'. The connection with Syr. kʷhad 'revere' is modest suggested by Brockelmann 1928:234 (followed by Drower-Macuch 205 for Mandaic) is unlikely. The root kʷhd is related to Ar. gaḫada 'negate, deny' (Dillmann 824). ETHIOPIC: Tna. kʷäḫadä 'deny', Te. kʷäḫada, Amh.Gur. kʷadä passed into Cushitic: Bil.Sa. kʷadä 'deny'. Qua. kʷād. Cerulli 1936:257 connects kʷhd with the root kʷd 'betray' (see kʷadʷa, above), however, kʷd is borrowed from Arabic.

phonetics: kʷäḫaka, kʷäḫaka, kʷäḫak hʷḫa i hʷḫa

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: onomatopoetic, related to Ar. qaqawa 'make noise', Aram. kʷkaʾ.

meaning: kʷäḫala hʷU 'use antimony for the eyelid, anoint the eyelid, (T.K.Li) mark with an iron, cauterize'; takʷäḫala, takʷäḫala, passive; also 'paint the eyelid (with antimony)'; takʷäḫala (DTW 648) 'paint one another's eyelids'; ʾastakʷäḫala (DTW 648), caus. of takʷäḫala; kʷäḫali (K*), act. part. of kʷäḫala; kʷäḫl 'antimony, dye for the eyelid'; kʷäḫl 'blue'; ʾar kʷäḫl 'antimony for the eyelid';
origin_explanation: from Ar. kʷuḫl 'antimony' (Noeldeke 1910:40 thinks that G. kʷuḫl is of Aram.-Syr. kʷuḫlā origin); also in Tna. kʷuḫlā 'apply antimony', Te. kʷuḫala, Amh. kʷuḫlā, Gur. (Tä)kʷalā 'antimony'; Te. kʷuḫal, Amh. kʷuḫal, Gur. kʷuḫl. According to Zimmern 61 the origin of this root is Akk. guḫlu 'antimony' > Aram.-Syr. kʷuḫlā, Md. kʷula > Heb. kʷuḫal 'paint the eyelid', Ph. kʷuḫl 'antimony', Ar. kʷuḫl > alcohol (Lo kotsch, no. 1227).

meaning: Tna. kʷaḫ bälä 'make noise (a falling object)', Te. kʷaḫaḫ bälä 'make noise', Amh. kʷaḫ kʷaḫ 'crack, explode', ʾaskʷaḫa 'neigh, cluck', Har. kʷaḫkʷaḫ 'sound of knocking';
origin_explanation: also Cushitic: Af. kʷaḫ.

phonetics: kʷäḫkʷaḫa hʷNḫhʷ (instead of kʷaḫkʷaḫa of K*)
meaning: 'mix, grind coarsely, break open (a seed), crash'; ʾankʷaḫkʷaḫa (T.H. should read ʾankʷäḫkʷaḫa) 'crumble up bread and mix it with sauce, water, butter, and so on'. (It is strange that M quoting T renders it 'trot'; one gets the impression that M mistakenly translated the line above, namely, G. ʾanbaḫbaḫa = Amh. dubb dubb alä 'trot'); kʷäḫkʷaḫat (M) 'dish made of small pieces of meat cooked in a sauce'; nakʷäḫkʷaḫ (instead of nakʷaḫkʷaḫ of K*) 'dish made of crumbled-up bread, sauce, butter, and so on, dregs or residue floating on the surface of a beverage';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. kʷakka 'grind coarsely, smash the head', Te. kʷaḫ ʾabälä 'hit the head making a wound'.

ETHIOPIC: Tna. kähasä 'compensate', Te. kähasa, Amh. kasä, Arg. kähasa, Har. kähäsa; passed into Cushitic: Aw. kasu. Note that Praetorius 1879:7 does not connect this root with Heb. kähas 'grow lean', as suggested by Appleyard 50.
SEMITIC: Heb. kähas 'grow lean' (so also Praetorius 1879:7), Aram. kahās. ETHIOPIC: related to Amh. kässa 'be thin' (from ks²), kʷäššäsa, Tna. kʷäššäsä, Arg. kʷäššäha.

meaning: 'be inflamed (wound), fester (wound), (T.T.S.H) be large (wound), ooze (wound), (K) be superficially healed'; kʷahs (T.M) 'inflammation, (T.M) widening of a wound, (K) woman's genitalia'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. kʷäshe (with metathesis) 'become large (wound)'.

SEMITIC: onomatopoetic: Ar. kākā 'cluck (hen), cackle, chatter' (Dozy II, 435). ETHIOPIC: Te. kak bela 'croak', käkay 'raven', Tna. kʷak bela 'croak', kʷak 'raven', Amh. kʷak, call or cry made by a chicken, (tän)kʷakkʷa 'clatter', (as) kʷakka 'cluck'. See also Gur. ka balä, kʷa balä, kaka III (Leslau 1979:331, 340). The root also occurs in Cushitic: Sa. Af. käk 'shout, noise', Sa. käko 'raven', Af. kakō, Bil. kʷikʷay. See also Gugä 'raven', qʷär I.

meaning: 'scarlet, purple, (OT) black felt cloak with an attached hood, (T) a red worm that produces scarlet color'
origin_explanation: from Gk. kokkos 'grain, seed, scarlet'.
from Ar. hawkh(a) 'plum, peach' (for its Aramaic origin, see Fraenkel 142); also in Te.Amh. kok 'peach', Tna. kuk, Gur. kuk, Har. xūxi; in Cushitic: Sa. kak 'peach', Bil. kok. See also kokah, hoh.

meaning: 'star'; (kʷokab zabottu zanab 'comet', lit. 'a star that has a tail')

origin_explanation: Sär. kʷkʷb, Mh. kʷokʷib, Soq. kʷibšib (with ki kʷkʷb, Aram. kōkābā, Syr. kawkabā, Mg. kʷkʷb, Ph. kkb, Akk. kakkabu. Moscati, Biblica 27 (1946).269-72 connects the noun with the root kbb 'shine' (Akk. kabābu 'burn'). For a bibliography on the etymology, see Baumgartner 441-2. ETHIOPIC: Te.Amh.Arg. kʷokab 'star', Tna. kʷokob, Gaf. kʷokʷobä, Gur. kʷkʷäb.

meaning: 'stone, rock, stony ground'; (abn kʷakʷah 'flinty rock'); in construct state 'stony'; e.g. garähta kʷakʷah 'stony field'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. kāh, kīh 'rugged face of a mountain, side of a valley consisting of the hardest and roughest stone'. Dillmann 859 (so also Koehler 430, Baumgartner 447) suggests Heb. ko²h 'strength'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. käwhi, kʷäkʷhi 'rock', Amh. kʷäkʷa 'stony place', kʷäkʷa 'hit with a rock', kʷäkʷa (from Geez); also in Cushitic: Sa. kakʷah 'rock'.
see kok.
misprint for kelul hʷl-A; see kallala I.

phonetics: (T), kakam hʷg (M), hʷg (Gr.266), kokamä hʷg (L), pl. kʷkamät, kokamät, kokamät (D)
meaning: 'hollowed gourd used for liquids, bowl, pan, (K.T.) scoop removing ashes, potsherd (for borrowing coals for a fire), (Gr.266) cauldron'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Fraenkel 70 derives it, with reservations, from Ar. qumqum 'long-necked bottle'. Noeldeke 1910:41 suggests Syr. qūmā 'jar'.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. kakkämä.

meaning: 'a measure of time, the sixtieth part of the day, (Gr.207) interval of twenty-four minutes'
origin_explanation: the derivation from Gk. kirkos, Lat. circus, suggested by Dillmann 859 is not accepted by Neugebauer 175. For more details on G. kikros, kekros, see Neugebauer 175-7.

meaning: 'turn' (intr); ankʷkʷawa 'roll around, make roll'; mankʷokʷot, pl. mankʷkut (D.T), mankʷokʷt mankʷakt (KG) 'axis, wheel, rotation, spindle' (note that KG I mankʷakt = Amh. kʷkʷat 'thumb' is copied by mistake from mankʷokʷat Amh. awratatʷo²e 'thumbs' of the line above)
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. (tän)kʷäkʷäkʷä 'run here and there, rush around', kʷäwʷa 'wandering'.

meaning: 'break or chip the set the teeth on edge (sour fruit)'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. (a)kʷokʷäyä 'set the teeth on edge'.
perhaps from Ar. hall 'old and worn-out garment'; also Amh. käll.
from Amh. käll, of Arabic (hall) origin; also in Te. käll.
kʷalä hʷa; see kʷawwala.

meaning: 'all, every, each'; without suffix pronouns in kʷallähe, kʷallahe (see below); kʷallu is also used with a feminine singular or with a plural noun; e.g. kʷallu mangäst 'the whole kingdom', kʷallu mawä²aliihu 'all his days'; kʷallu (adv.) 'altogether, completely'; with a negative 'not at all'; kʷallähe, kʷallähe 'everywhere, in every direction, wherever'; bakʷallähe, bakʷallähe 'everywhere, wherever, in every way, completely'; amkʷallähe, amkʷallähe 'from everywhere, from all parts'; kʷallawi 'universal'; kʷalläntä 'totality, entirety, the whole person'; with suffix pronouns 'all together, the whole of, wholly'; e.g., kʷalläntähu (Gr. also kʷallun-tähu) 'he wholly, the whole of him, all of it'; also with a noun: kʷalläntähä lelita 'the whole night', kʷalläntähu sägaka 'the whole of thy body'; kʷalläntäwi 'total'; kʷalläntäwit 'the whole person'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. kull 'all', Sär. kʷll 'all, every', Soq. kol 'every', Heb. Aram. kol, Syr. kul, Md. kul, Ph.Ug. kl, Akk. kullatu 'all, the totality'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. kʷall-u 'all', Te. kʷol, Gur. kʷull-əm, Har. kull, Amh. hullu, Gaf. al-əm.
transcription of Gk. kolla 'glue'.
from Ar. kʷula 'fruit dans l'Inde qui ressemble à l'orange' (Dozy II, p. 501); also Amh. kʷol.
undoubtedly wrongly for elul hA even though elul is not the eighth month. See also kalil.

meaning: 'hinder, prohibit, forbid, prevent, keep back, hold back, deprive, restrain, impede, decline, reject, refuse'; takalʷa, passive; also 'abstain, restrain oneself, hold oneself back, refrain, withdraw'; kalʷi, act. part. of kalʷa; kalʷu, pass. part. of kalʷa; kalʷi 'prohibition'; kalʷat 'restraint, hindrance, prohibition, prevention'

phonetics: makalla, maklā'i (LLK), act. part. of kal'a
meaning: also 'adversary, opponent'; maklā'i (Ll) maklā'i 'hindrance, impediment'; (zavalbo makla' 'indisputable'); maklā'i (K*) 'that which prevents someone from entering (gate, enclosure)'; takal(a)'ot 'abstinence, impediment, restraint, hindrance, obstacle, prohibition'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. kallā'a 'retain, detain', Soq. kole 'turn away', Heb. kālā' 'restrain, keep from', Aram.-Syr. kalā', Md. kla 'hold back, withhold', Akk. kalû 'detain, hold back, refuse'. ETHIOPIC: Te. kāl'a 'hinder, prevent', Tna. kāl'e, Amh.Gur. kālla; also in Cushitic: Sa. Af. kal, kale 'hold back'. See also kalkala.
would it mean 'go or run like a dog'? If so it would be a denominative from kalb.

meaning: 'dog'; takallaba 'have a canine nature'; takalbotannā 'canine nature'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. kalb 'dog', Sar. klb, Soq. klb, köb (with l > zero), Heb. keleb, Aram.-Syr. Md. kalba, Ph.Ug. klb, Akk. kalbu, denominative; Ar. kaliba 'behave like a rabid dog', Syr. kalb. ETHIOPIC: Te. kālb, Tna. kālbi. See also kalala.

from Gk. khalbāné 'galbanum', of Semitic origin (Masson 60); also in Amh. kalbāne. In the other Semitic languages: Ar. halbanā 'storax', Heb. helbana, PbH. helbōn, Aram. hilbēntā, helbānā, Syr. helbānā, Md. hilbuna. See also qalbāne.
from Gk. khelidōn 'swallow'.

Chaldea being considered the seat of astrology. Gk. khaldaion, acc. sg. of khaldaios 'astrologer'. SEMITIC: Ar. kaldānī 'astrologer', Aram. kaldā'ā 'Chaldean, soothsayer', Syr. kaldāyā, Md. kaldaia. On Kaldu: Kaśdîm, see Steiner 137-143.
from Ar. halīfa; also in Tna.Te.Amh. kālīfa 'disciple of a sheik'.

meaning: 'cry out, shout, cry aloud, howl'; 'akallaha, causative'; takallaha, passive
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. kālla 'take off the clothes', also in Cushitic: Sa. kalā', Af. kal. The root is related to ql (see qala'a). Guidi Aethiops 1 (1922). 51 has kal'a and he derives it from Ar. hala'a 'take off'.

meaning: 'shout to or at one another'; vastakāllaha 'roar, shout, cause to shout at one another, cause to cheer one another'; kalāhi, act. part. of kalha
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Har. kālaha, kalha 'call someone by shouting'. Amh. (a)kālla 'make a thundering noise (a reduplicated a-stem of kālla, root kl?), and probably also kāllalā 'resound' (formed by reduplication of the 2nd radical), Te. kālah 'little bell', (tā)kalhe 'argue with one another, quarrel'; probably passed into Cushitic: Sa. kallāh, Aw. kālāx-xā 'shout', Be. kalā 'bell'.

meaning: 'puncture, cut a vein in the forehead in order to bleed or cup a person'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. kālha 'incise the skin on the forehead so that blood flows out', Tna. kālhahe, Gur. k'ōlāk'ālā (reduplicated klh > kl > klkl) 'scratch the ground (hen, cock, bull), make little holes'.

meaning: 'hinder, prevent, prohibit'; takalkala, passive'; kalkāli (Lt), act. part. of kalkala
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: reduplicated verb of kl coming from (see kal'a): Te. kālkāla 'hinder, keep back', kālkāla, Amh.Gaf. kālakkālā, Arg. kālakkālā, (tā)klakkālā.

meaning: 'two, make another, (L) change, alter (denominative)'; kal'e 'two, both, double, twofold'; with suffix pronouns: 'both of..., two of...'; (ba-kal'e 'twofold'); kal'ettu (acc. kal'etta) 'two (with masc. and fem. nouns), both, double'; kal'etti 'two (with fem. nouns), both'; kal'e (fem. kal'o) 'other, another, anyone else, second, successor, companion, friend, neighbor'; (kāl'a lakāl'a'u 'one to another'); (kāl'a 'amkāl'a'u 'one more than another'); kāl'a 'again'; kāl'e'it (Lt) 'second section of a day'; kāl'a'āwi (fem. kāl'āwit) 'second'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. kālā'a 'both', Sar. kly, Soq. kalā, kalā, Heb. kil-'ayim 'of two kinds', Ug. kla-t 'both', Akk. kilallû (m). For more details on the Arabic root, see Rabin 69. ETHIOPIC: Te. kal'ot 'two', Tna. kal'aite (root kl), Har. k'ot, k'ot (l > zero), Arg. ket, Gur. k'ett, h'ett, Amh. hulätt, Gaf. alatta (k > h, zero), Tna. kal'a 'another'. Amh. also kālla 'to double, make two' (from G. kal'e); passed into Cushitic: Som. kall 'another'. Rabin 69 seems to connect this root with kl 'restrain' (see kal'a, above) with an original meaning 'yoke'.

meaning: 'pull out (the weeds), peel off, undress, strip off, remove (Lt), break, dislocate'

origin_explanation: from Gk. glykiston 'sweetest', from glykys 'sweet', or from glykysma 'sweetness' (Müller).

meaning: 'surround, surround for protection, cover over, protect, encompass, encircle, fence in, crown'; 'akallala (K*), causative'; takallala, pass. of kallala; kallāli (K*), act. part. of kallala; kallul, pass. part. of kallala; kallālē 'crowning, coronation, surrounding, encompassing'; 'aklīl 'crown, diadem, garland, tiara, headdress worn by the deacons at the liturgy, (Lt) ban'
origin_explanation: makallal (Gr) 'who crowns'; 'aklīl 'coronation, church wedding, nuptial ceremony (reserved for those who marry the first time)'; SEMITIC: Ar. kallala 'to crown', takallala 'be crowned' are denominatives. As for Ar. 'aklīl 'crown' and G. 'aklīl, they are considered to be of Aramaic origin (Fraenkel 62), as is Heb. kallīlā (Wagner 64). On the Akkadian (kalīlu) origin of this root, see Zimmern 36, von Soden 476. See also on this root Kaufman 63-4. For kll being the origin of Sem. kul(l) 'all', see Baumgartner 457. Hebrew kallā 'bride' (for the other Semitic languages, see Baumgartner 455) is perhaps also connected with this root. ETHIOPIC: Te. kāllala (kll) 'go around', 'akāllala 'encircle', kalal 'circuit', 'aklīl 'crown', Tna. k'allalā 'go around', 'aklīl 'crown', tāklīl 'sacrament of indissoluble marriage', Amh. kāllalā 'surround, crown, guard, protect', 'aklīl 'crown', tāklīl 'sacrament of indissoluble marriage' (see Walker 36ff); passed into Cushitic: Sa. kulel 'circle', Bil. kalāl.

meaning: 'cut, (M) decree, grant, (T.Y.K) thank'; kallil (Lt) 'who cuts, who assigns'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. kāllala (kll) 'examine', 'akāllal 'the separation of good and bad', Amh. kāllalā 'demarcate, delimit a boundary, set the limits'. Note that the meaning 'thank' could also be derived from kallala I 'crown' > express thanks'.
corrupt form of 'elul hāñ, from Heb. 'elūl even though 'elūl is not the fourth month. See also kul.

meaning: 'roll (intr), roll down (tears), fall (fruit, tears), be (become) giddy, be tossed about, turn (one's head; tr. intr), make giddy'; 'akolala (Gr.266) 'be dizzy'; nakolāl 'dizziness, giddiness, spinning of the head, (Gr.221) agitation, excitement; lock, curl, tuft of hair' (< 'turn'); nakulāl (T.M), nakawall (L) 'who loses his power of reasoning by being inebriated, one whose head turns (due to drunkenness, or for any reason)'; mankolāl (Lt) 'dizziness'; nakul, see below;

phonetics: makalla, maklā'i (LLK), act. part. of kal'a
meaning: also 'adversary, opponent'; maklā'i (Ll) maklā'i 'hindrance, impediment'; (zavalbo makla' 'indisputable'); maklā'i (K*) 'that which prevents someone from entering (gate, enclosure)'; takal(a)'ot 'abstinence, impediment, restraint, hindrance, obstacle, prohibition'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. kallā'a 'retain, detain', Soq. kole 'turn away', Heb. kālā' 'restrain, keep from', Aram.-Syr. kalā', Md. kla 'hold back, withhold', Akk. kalû 'detain, hold back, refuse'. ETHIOPIC: Te. kāl'a 'hinder, prevent', Tna. kāl'e, Amh.Gur. kālla; also in Cushitic: Sa. Af. kal, kale 'hold back'. See also kalkala.
would it mean 'go or run like a dog'? If so it would be a denominative from kalb.

meaning: 'dog'; takallaba 'have a canine nature'; takalbotannā 'canine nature'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. kalb 'dog', Sar. klb, Soq. klb, köb (with l > zero), Heb. keleb, Aram.-Syr. Md. kalba, Ph.Ug. klb, Akk. kalbu, denominative; Ar. kaliba 'behave like a rabid dog', Syr. kalb. ETHIOPIC: Te. kālb, Tna. kālbi. See also kalala.

from Gk. khalbāné 'galbanum', of Semitic origin (Masson 60); also in Amh. kalbāne. In the other Semitic languages: Ar. halbanā 'storax', Heb. helbana, PbH. helbōn, Aram. hilbēntā, helbānā, Syr. helbānā, Md. hilbuna. See also qalbāne.
from Gk. khelidōn 'swallow'.

Chaldea being considered the seat of astrology. Gk. khaldaion, acc. sg. of khaldaios 'astrologer'. SEMITIC: Ar. kaldānī 'astrologer', Aram. kaldā'ā 'Chaldean, soothsayer', Syr. kaldāyā, Md. kaldaia. On Kaldu: Kaśdîm, see Steiner 137-143.
from Ar. halīfa; also in Tna.Te.Amh. kālīfa 'disciple of a sheik'.

meaning: 'cry out, shout, cry aloud, howl'; 'akallaha, causative'; takallaha, passive
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. kālla 'take off the clothes', also in Cushitic: Sa. kalā', Af. kal. The root is related to ql (see qala'a). Guidi Aethiops 1 (1922). 51 has kal'a and he derives it from Ar. hala'a 'take off'.

meaning: 'shout to or at one another'; vastakāllaha 'roar, shout, cause to shout at one another, cause to cheer one another'; kalāhi, act. part. of kalha
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Har. kālaha, kalha 'call someone by shouting'. Amh. (a)kālla 'make a thundering noise (a reduplicated a-stem of kālla, root kl?), and probably also kāllalā 'resound' (formed by reduplication of the 2nd radical), Te. kālah 'little bell', (tā)kalhe 'argue with one another, quarrel'; probably passed into Cushitic: Sa. kallāh, Aw. kālāx-xā 'shout', Be. kalā 'bell'.

meaning: 'puncture, cut a vein in the forehead in order to bleed or cup a person'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. kālha 'incise the skin on the forehead so that blood flows out', Tna. kālhahe, Gur. k'ōlāk'ālā (reduplicated klh > kl > klkl) 'scratch the ground (hen, cock, bull), make little holes'.

meaning: 'hinder, prevent, prohibit'; takalkala, passive'; kalkāli (Lt), act. part. of kalkala
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: reduplicated verb of kl coming from (see kal'a): Te. kālkāla 'hinder, keep back', kālkāla, Amh.Gaf. kālakkālā, Arg. kālakkālā, (tā)klakkālā.

meaning: 'two, make another, (L) change, alter (denominative)'; kal'e 'two, both, double, twofold'; with suffix pronouns: 'both of..., two of...'; (ba-kal'e 'twofold'); kal'ettu (acc. kal'etta) 'two (with masc. and fem. nouns), both, double'; kal'etti 'two (with fem. nouns), both'; kal'e (fem. kal'o) 'other, another, anyone else, second, successor, companion, friend, neighbor'; (kāl'a lakāl'a'u 'one to another'); (kāl'a 'amkāl'a'u 'one more than another'); kāl'a 'again'; kāl'e'it (Lt) 'second section of a day'; kāl'a'āwi (fem. kāl'āwit) 'second'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. kālā'a 'both', Sar. kly, Soq. kalā, kalā, Heb. kil-'ayim 'of two kinds', Ug. kla-t 'both', Akk. kilallû (m). For more details on the Arabic root, see Rabin 69. ETHIOPIC: Te. kal'ot 'two', Tna. kal'aite (root kl), Har. k'ot, k'ot (l > zero), Arg. ket, Gur. k'ett, h'ett, Amh. hulätt, Gaf. alatta (k > h, zero), Tna. kal'a 'another'. Amh. also kālla 'to double, make two' (from G. kal'e); passed into Cushitic: Som. kall 'another'. Rabin 69 seems to connect this root with kl 'restrain' (see kal'a, above) with an original meaning 'yoke'.

meaning: 'pull out (the weeds), peel off, undress, strip off, remove (Lt), break, dislocate'

origin_explanation: from Gk. glykiston 'sweetest', from glykys 'sweet', or from glykysma 'sweetness' (Müller).

meaning: 'surround, surround for protection, cover over, protect, encompass, encircle, fence in, crown'; 'akallala (K*), causative'; takallala, pass. of kallala; kallāli (K*), act. part. of kallala; kallul, pass. part. of kallala; kallālē 'crowning, coronation, surrounding, encompassing'; 'aklīl 'crown, diadem, garland, tiara, headdress worn by the deacons at the liturgy, (Lt) ban'
origin_explanation: makallal (Gr) 'who crowns'; 'aklīl 'coronation, church wedding, nuptial ceremony (reserved for those who marry the first time)'; SEMITIC: Ar. kallala 'to crown', takallala 'be crowned' are denominatives. As for Ar. 'aklīl 'crown' and G. 'aklīl, they are considered to be of Aramaic origin (Fraenkel 62), as is Heb. kallīlā (Wagner 64). On the Akkadian (kalīlu) origin of this root, see Zimmern 36, von Soden 476. See also on this root Kaufman 63-4. For kll being the origin of Sem. kul(l) 'all', see Baumgartner 457. Hebrew kallā 'bride' (for the other Semitic languages, see Baumgartner 455) is perhaps also connected with this root. ETHIOPIC: Te. kāllala (kll) 'go around', 'akāllala 'encircle', kalal 'circuit', 'aklīl 'crown', Tna. k'allalā 'go around', 'aklīl 'crown', tāklīl 'sacrament of indissoluble marriage', Amh. kāllalā 'surround, crown, guard, protect', 'aklīl 'crown', tāklīl 'sacrament of indissoluble marriage' (see Walker 36ff); passed into Cushitic: Sa. kulel 'circle', Bil. kalāl.

meaning: 'cut, (M) decree, grant, (T.Y.K) thank'; kallil (Lt) 'who cuts, who assigns'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. kāllala (kll) 'examine', 'akāllal 'the separation of good and bad', Amh. kāllalā 'demarcate, delimit a boundary, set the limits'. Note that the meaning 'thank' could also be derived from kallala I 'crown' > express thanks'.
corrupt form of 'elul hāñ, from Heb. 'elūl even though 'elūl is not the fourth month. See also kul.

meaning: 'roll (intr), roll down (tears), fall (fruit, tears), be (become) giddy, be tossed about, turn (one's head; tr. intr), make giddy'; 'akolala (Gr.266) 'be dizzy'; nakolāl 'dizziness, giddiness, spinning of the head, (Gr.221) agitation, excitement; lock, curl, tuft of hair' (< 'turn'); nakulāl (T.M), nakawall (L) 'who loses his power of reasoning by being inebriated, one whose head turns (due to drunkenness, or for any reason)'; mankolāl (Lt) 'dizziness'; nakul, see below;

'hoop, wheel', Tna. kolāliə 'go around', ənkəlīl bālā 'surround', ənkəlīlw 'round', Amh. (ṭān)k°əllalā, (an)k°əllalālā, k°əllalā alā 'roll, make turn', Har. kulul bāya 'roll', Gur. (aṭ)kulāle. See also kawlala.

'hobble (domestic animal), stammer (that is, be tongue-tied)', Gur. (ṭā)k°əllāṭāfā 'stammer'. The root is augmented by l in relation to kaṭafa (see below). It is strange that it is similar in meaning to kalṭəha, below.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh.
from Gr. khlamyda (acc. of khlamys 'cloak'); also in Amh. kələməda 'red silk, red velvet' (from Geez), Syr. kaləmida. For Gk. khlamys, cp. Aram. kalmūs, kalmūs 'cloak', Syr. kalmūs.
could it be borrowed from Ar. kalimāt 'words'? (Ambros); cp. also Te. kaləmāṭa 'incite to fight by praising speeches', kaləmaṭ 'song'.
see kamelewon I.
kalqədonāwi (Lt) 'pertaining to chalcedony'; from Gk. khalkēdōn; also Amh. kelqədon (from Geez), Syr. qalīḍūn.

meaning: 'bind sheaves'; takalsasa (K*), passive; kalsəsṭ (pl. kalsəsāt, kalāsəsṭ) 'sheaf, bundle'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Dillmann 818 compares Ar. kalaza 'gather' > kls by partial assimilation (for which see Brockelmann 1908:153). ETHIOPIC: Tna. kəlsāsä 'bind the sheaves'.

SEMITIC: Ar. kulyā 'kidney', Soq. keloih 'intestines', Heb. kilyā 'kidney', Aram.-Syr. kulyā, Md. kulai, Ug. klyt, Akk. kalītu. ETHIOPIC: Tna. k°əlīt 'kidney', Te. kəlkəl°ot, kəlwat, kəlawa. Amh. k°əlalīt, Gur. kulalīt, kilāyo, Gaf. kullalīt, Arg. kullay. Har. kulay; also in Cushitic: Bil. kilbīt, Som. kelī, Or kalē; see also Sasse 12.
see also kalṭəwa, kalṭafa.

meaning: (T.Y.Sw. in D 1397) 'bind, tie'; takalṭafa (Lt), passive; kalṭuf (T) 'bound, tied'.

origin_explanation: see also k°āləwa, mak°āləwa.

seems to be a reconstructed form from makliṭ 'tal (weight)'. SEMITIC: Dillmann 822 compares it with Ar. kāla (kyl) 'measure', Aram. Heb. kāl (kwl, kyl) 'contain', Aram. kāl 'measure', Syr. kayl 'a measure'. For G. makliṭ, see Pankhurst 1970, 1:55.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. kam, same (from Geez) perhaps an interpretation of the biblical Ham, one of Noah's sons, referring to blackness.

meaning: (adverb, preposition, and conjunc-tion) 'as, just as, even as, like, as if, such'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. kamā 'like, as', Heb. kəmō, Aram. kəmā, Ug.Ph. km, Akk. kima; it represents Semitic k 'like' with -ma, -mā, conceived either as an interrogative or as an indefinite particle (Brockelmann 1908:326). ETHIOPIC: Tna. kām 'like, as, according to'. Te. kəm, Amh. kəmā (from Geez), Arg. kəmāy 'how' (lit. 'like what?'), Gur (yä)-kəma, (yä)-kəm 'like'.
from Aram.-Syr. kīmā, Heb. kīmā (Noeldeke 1910:45), Md. kima; also in Te. kema; passed into Cushitic: Be. hēma, Bil.Sa. kaymāt. Kaufman 64 connects it with Akk. kimtu 'family'.

meaning: 'thus, also, even, nearly, almost, just, merely, alone, the same, yet'
(T) komal hənə (Lt) 'kind of tree (rendered by T as Amh. əše 'tree of the hot lowlands which has edible berries')'.

origin_explanation: from Gk. khamaileōn 'chameleon'; also Aram. kamele'ōn. See also kelemewon, galāməwos.

meaning: (T+) = Amh. qammo 'a tree whose fruit grows in bunches'; could it be the same as komol?
origin_explanation: could it be the same as komol?
ETHIOPIC: Amh. kəmāmā, kəmākkəmā, kārākəmam 'trim', Tna. kəmākəmā 'equalize by cutting, trim', kārkəmā (by dissimilation), Te. kəmākəmā 'put in order'.

meaning: (K.Lt) 'fine vestment worn by an officiating priest, embroidered cuffs, maniple' (Brightman 591; Hammerschmidt 1970:153); from Ar. kumm (pl. əkmām) 'sleeve'.
origin_explanation: from Ar. kumm (pl. əkmām) 'sleeve'.

from Gk. kyminon 'cumin', possibly through Ar. kammūn; also in Te. kamun, Tna.Amh. kāmūn, Har. kammūn, kummōn. SEMITIC: Heb. kammōn, Aram. kammōnā, Syr. kammūnā, Md. kamuna, Ph.Ug. kmn. For its Akkadian origin, see Zimmern 57. On Gk. kyminon in relation to Semitic, see Masson 51-2.
note that KG has kawānim 'provisions for a journey'. For the confusion kawānim: kamānim, see also kamānim II.
Dillmann 1406 considers it a corruption of Heb. (ədār)konim of Ezra 8:27. Note that KS26 and KG have kawānim, but the Asmara Bible has kamānim. For kawānim: kamānim, see also kamānim I.
perhaps from Gk. kyminon 'cumin' (Müller).

takamra (Gr 258), passive;kəmr, kəmr (pl. 'akmār) 'heap, pile';makmar (Lt) 'heap';SEMITIC: Ug. kmr 'pile', Akk. kamāru 'pile up'.ETHIOPIC: Te. kəmmāra 'pile up', Tna. kəmmārā, Amh. kəmmārā, Arg. kəmmārā, Gaf. kəmmārā, Gur. kəmmārā; passed into Cushitic: Sa.Af. kamar 'accumulate', Bil. kəmmār. For its connection with gmr (see gamara), see Cohen 1926-7:97, n. 2.
from Ar. ḥamr; also in Te. kāmṛ 'fermented drink', Amh. kāmṛ, Har. xamri.
transcription of Gk. khōmarim, from Heb. kəmārīm 'idolatrous priests'; also Chr.-Ar. kumar (from Syr. kumrā).
SEMITIC: perhaps Ar. kamasa 'be stern-looking'.

phonetics: kamos, kəmos, komos HPP (T.K), kəmos HPP (D 1406)
origin_explanation: transcription of Gk. khamōs, from Heb. kəmōš, the God of the Moabites.

meaning: 'religious clerk who assigns lodgings to believers gathered for a festivity'
origin_explanation: is to be identified with qomos, below.

akmosasa 'smile, deride, chide, rebuke in a mild manner, reproach';kamosəs, kamosās 'mild gravity, cheerfulness, smile' (n);kəmsəs (Gr.258) 'who smiles';ETHIOPIC: Te. kəmsəs bela 'smile', Tna. kəmass bälä, (-a) kəmäsmäsä.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. kəməʿəčč alä 'be short (cloth, garment, due to shrinkage).

meaning: 'suffix pronoun of the 2nd plural fem. with nouns, verbs, and particles'
origin_explanation: see -ka, -ki, -k(k)ə(k)mu, above.

kinat 'art, craft, handicraft, workmanship';kenəyā (pl. kenəyāt, kinat, kenāt) 'artisan, craftsman, fashioner, workman';kenəwi (pl. kenawi) 'skillful, accomplished in art, artistic, artificial';takenawa 'fabricate with skill, be skilled in, use a stratagem';takenəwo(t) 'handiwork, skill, work of art';SEMITIC: related to Ar. qayṇ 'blacksmith', qaynā 'beat iron', Aram. qēnay, qēnāwā 'metalworker, silversmith', Syr. qaynayā, Md. qanaia, Ug. qyn 'blacksmith'. On Heb. qayīn, see H. Rabin, 1974:315, 325.ETHIOPIC: Te. kin 'intention, will', Tna. kən, Amh. kin 'art', kenya 'skilled, artisan' (from Geez); also in Cushitic: Kham. kin 'custom, manners, way of life', Qua. kin. See also kəʿināt.

meaning: 'comb, pin, hook or ring (of a sandal)'
origin_explanation: is augmented by n in relation to kabalo, see above.

ETHIOPIC: Tna. kənbälä 'turn over', kəmbälbal bälä 'tumble down', Te. kəmbälä (< kənbəl) 'turn and flow out', Amh. kənbälbälä 'pour out (liquids), fall backward', kənbäl alä 'be turned over', Gur. ka-nəbälä 'return, bring back' (for the various meanings of the root in Gurage, see Leslau 1979:345); passed into Cushitic: Aw. kəmbəli 'roll'.
from Gk. koinobia, nom. acc. pl. of koinobion 'religious community, monastery'; cp. also Chr.-Ar. qanūbiyā 'cloister'. See also kabyon, above.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. kənāf, kənāf 'animal led by a rope passed through a nose ring'; see also Praetorius, ZDMG 64 (1910).623.

meaning: 'wing, fin (of fish), branch of tree, border (of garment, land)'
origin_explanation: kənafa (yaknaf) (K*) 'fly' (denominative);SEMITIC: Ar. kanaf 'wing', Sar. knf 'border', side', Heb. kānāp 'wing', Aram. kənap, Syr. kənap, Md. kanpa, Ug. knp, Akk. kappu.ETHIOPIC: Te.Amh.Arg. kənf 'wing', Tna. kənf, Gur. kənfa, Amh. also (tä)kənāfənāfä 'fly, flutter about'; common with Cushitic: Qab. kənf 'a wing', Bil. kanfe, Kham. Kuff (see on this root Dolgopolsky 65). Conti Rossini 1912:214 also compares Qem. kämbi.
possibly in connection with kənf 'border', with reduplicated last radical.

Lagarde 231 compares Ar. kinfira 'tip of the nose'. In this case knfr would indicate the extremity of a part of the body, that is, the relation to the mouth, and the tip of the nose in relation to the nose. The root would then be common with Cushitic rather than borrowed from Cushitic (Bil. kanfar, Qua. kanpar) as suggested by Bravmann 508f is not convincing. The connection with Mh. karfif 'lip', Sh. qifrir suggested by Bittner 1916:29 is unlikely.ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna.Amh.Gur. kənfār 'lip'.

phonetics: kənfār HPP C (T.Sw. in D 404, top), kənfār HPP C (T)
meaning: 'hump, arm, front of leg of an animal'

origin_explanation: from Gk. konkylion, neuter of konkylios 'purple colored'; G. kənk 'al could probably mean 'garment dyed purple'.

meaning: 'regulate rule, over, have dominion, govern, judge, pass judgment, pass sentence, plead, convict, condemn, punish, avenge, torture, vex, chastise, exercise power'
origin_explanation: 'ak'annana, causative; also 'appoint a judge, give authority over, establish, put in charge of';tak'annana, pass. of kən'anna; also 'be executed, be subject to power, serve';(tak'annāni 'punished');tak'anna 'plead, carry on a lawsuit, litigate, press for justice, become reconciled';'astak'annana 'let appear for judgment, let appear in court';kən'annāni, act. part. of kən'anna; also 'ruler, governor';kən'annun, pass. part. of kən'anna;kən'annäne 'rule, domain, sentence, judgment, trial, law, ordinance, damnation, condemnation, doom, punishment, torment, torture';(kən'annäne fatḥ 'judicial punishment');kən'annəno 'judgment';kən'annat 'judgment, judging, condemnation';kən'annanāwi, (M) kən'annaneyāwi 'judicial';mak'annən (pl. mak'annən) 'ruler, prince, governor, magistrate, officer, high official, headman, prefect, judge, noble, nobleman, dignitary';mak'annən 'court of justice, hall of judgment, judgment seat, office, place of punishment, tribunal, jurisdiction, judgment, dominion, government, governorship, authority, territory subjected to the jurisdiction of';tak'annənot 'lawsuit';SEMITIC: The comparison with Heb. kōnen 'establish' suggested by Grimme 1901:424 is unlikely since Heb. kōnen is a pōlel-stem of ken (kwn) One might perhaps identify G. kən'ann with Semitic kwn (Heb. kēn, Syr. kawwen 'erect, admonish') with reduplicated last radical, without comparing it with the Hebrew pōlel-stem. The connection between mak'annən and Soq. qaninḥin 'master' suggested by Rabin 1978:400 is possible.ETHIOPIC: Te. kənnāna 'judge', Tna.Amh. kən'annänä 'judge, punish, condemn', passed into Cushitic: Sa. Bil. kənən 'judge'.
from Syr. kānūn; also Chr.-Ar. kānūn, in the expression 'first kānūn = December; second kānūn = January'.

SEMITIC: probably to be identified with kin 'art, workmanship'. The connection with Heb. ḥānīṭ 'spear' suggested by Dillmann 870, Cohen 1947: no. 254 and others, is unlikely. As for Heb. ḥānīṭ, it is considered an Egyptian (ḥnyṭ) loanword (Buhl 244, Baumgartner 320). Brockelmann 1950:16 considers G. k"ināṭ a Kafa (g"inā) loanword. ETHIOPIC: Te. konat 'spear', Tna. k"anat. See also kanaya.
SEMITIC: related to Ar. qanṭara 'arched vaulted passage, arch' (Ambros).

meaning: (Gr.267), pl. kantarōsāt, kəntərōsāt (D) 'cycle, a fourth part of the year, (Gr.267) interval of two minutes'.
origin_explanation: Neugebauer 178 states that kantaros indicates 1/30. from Gk. kentron, name of a parameter (see Neugebauer 175, 177-8).

meaning: (acc. kanto) 'nothingness, empty thing, emptiness, vanity, vain thing, vain, useless'; in the construct state 'vain, empty'; e.g., nagara kantu 'vain words, empty words'; kanto, bakantu, lakantu 'without reason, fortuitously, without purpose, without a cause, in vain, for nothing, unjustly, gratis, freely'.
origin_explanation: Dillmann 1907:363 analyzes kantu as a noun kant with the suffix pronoun -u of the 3rd pers. sg. masculine. SEMITIC: the connection with Aram. kaḍū 'without special reason' suggested by Barth 1893:40 is doubtful. Note that Aram. kaḍī is explained by Levy II:296 as coming from kaḍūb 'falseness'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. Amh.Gur. (bä)kantu 'without reason'; passed into Cushitic: Qem.Qua. kantu. The root is not to be connected with Te. kändo 'perhaps' as suggested by Brockelmann 1950:54. Likewise unlikely is the suggestion made by Praetorius 1879:336 that kantu) is an original bakant for bakat (with an inserted n), bakat being a feminine of bak 'useless, valueless' (for which see above).

meaning: (KG II, III; but KG I has wrongly kerotāṭ); see (kwn), kawāniṭ.
(yakni) (K*) 'parry a blow, fend off, ward off';
origin_explanation: seems to be a denominative from k"ināṭ 'shield' with the original meaning 'ward off with a shield'.

meaning: (MA) käp 'an over-vestment of priests'; in former times it had a hood, later the hood was removed and the käppä was lengthened to reach the ankles (see Guidi, ZA 11[1896].404; Hammerschmidt 1970:154);
origin_explanation: from Lat. cappa through Greek kappa 'hood'; in Tna.Amh. kabba, kappa, Gur. kubba; in Cushitic: Or. kabba.
probably a secondary form of qafo.
kup (MA) 'top of skull'; see also kup II.
could there be a graphic error for rop?
see also kop.
either from Gk. kyphi 'an Egyptian compound incense', of Egyptian (kšpt) origin, or from Coptic kōp 'fumigation' (Müller). See also (swp), sopa.

meaning: (originally 'knife'), name given a sect of the Ethiopian Orthodox church which followed an extreme interpretation of Monophysitism maintaining that Christ anointed himself. This sect referred to itself as (the Son [is] the unction'), but received the name kärrä 'knife' because it 'cut off' the human nature of Christ by declaring this to have been absorbed by the divine nature or because it reduced His nature to one like the kärrä-knife which has only a single edge (definition given by J.J. Baeteman, Dictionnaire amarigna-français, 1929, col. 717);
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: the derivation of kärrä from Sem. wqr 'punch' suggested by Praetorius 1879:72, 144-5 is unlikely. ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh. karra 'knife' (also the sect), Gaf. karrā, Har. karrā 'curved sword'; Cushitic: Or. kara. Kham. karrā.
transcription of Heb. kor 'dry measure'; see for this noun Kaufman 65.
H.G (Lt)
kora (K*) 'carry, transport a load' (denominative); from Ar. kīr 'saddle', also Te. kor, Amh. kor, korəčča; in Cushitic: Bil.Sa.Af.Be. kōr, Som. Or. kōra.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. k"arro 'jar', Amh. kora, Gur. kuro; also in Cushitic: Sa. kurro 'jar', Af. kōrā, Som. kōra. See also kore.

meaning: (K*) 'salt lick, water containing a mineral sought by animals for its salt content, pond';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: for 'pond'; cp. Amh. kure.

meaning: 'spout of a pitcher, mouth of a vessel'.
origin_explanation: see also korā I.
from Gk. khōrepiskopos 'country bishop'; also in Chr.-Ar. ḥūrābiskubūs, Syr. kūrēpīšqūpā.

meaning: (CO) (T) k"arra 'hit on the head with the fist (or palms), strike, smite, oppress';
origin_explanation: tak"arra 'passive'; k"arru (K*) 'struck (head), hit (head)'; k"arrāṭ 'striking, assault, reviling'; SEMITIC: related to Ar. qar'a 'knock (at a door), beat (a drum), strike on the head' (Dillmann 840; Grimme 1901:476). ETHIOPIC: Tna. k"ärra 'abālā 'hit on the head', Amh. k"ärra. See also qara.
k"arru (DTW) 'proud, arrogant'; k"arrāṭ 'arrogance'; ETHIOPIC: Tna. k"ärre 'be proud, be conceited', Amh. k"ärra; passed into Cushitic: Kham. k"ər 'be arrogant', Or. kōra 'pride'.

SEMITIC: Ar. karaba 'fasten tightly', perhaps SAr. (h)krb, (s)krb 'unite a bride with one's family' (Müller); ETHIOPIC: Te. kåräba 'tie up'. Amh. kårråbä 'hang, suspend from a hook'. See also k°ärbat.

meaning: 'hew (wood, stones), carve, (K) dig, plow, destroy'; mak°rabi (T.K.*M) 'plow, axe';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. karaba 'plow' (for a possible Aram.-Syr. [karab] origin, see Fraenkel 127), Md. krb. See also krp.

SEMITIC: Buhl 347 (followed by others) compares Heb. kəlûb 'basket', PBh. kalîbā; Syr. kulbāšā 'basket for carrying grapes' seems to be derived from Gk. klobos 'bird cage' rather than the other way around. See also M. Cohen 1926-7:105. A. Beeston, Muséon 91 (1978), 197 connects the root of 'woven basket' with mək°räb 'gathering place' from a meaning 'tie, bind, unite', but 'woven basket' is karabo and not karab as given by Beeston, ib. See also kabaro II. ETHIOPIC: Tna. karibbo 'small skin used as a bag', Amh. kåråbo 'basket'.
ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna.Amh.Gur. kårbe 'myrrh', Har. kåråbi; also in Cushitic: Bil. karbi, Sa. karbe.
a misprint for k°aräki hčh, below.
from Gk. kheroub, transcription of Heb. kərûb.
transcription of Gk. khōrēb (from Heb. horeb) 'the mountain of Horeb' which is the mountain of legislation, according to Deuteronomy.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. kurb, same (from Geez).
from kirub with the ending -el, probably under the influence of the names of angels such as mika'el, gabra'el.
SEMITIC: perhaps from karaba 'hang up', amulets being worn around the neck; or to be compared with SAr. krb 'blessing, favor'.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. kåräbeza, same.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. kåräddädä 'be rough'.

kardädäwäyän, in the pl. kardädäwäyän 'one who arouses dissension', derived from an original meaning 'covered with weeds' > bad > vicious'; ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna. kårdad 'weed, nettle', Amh. kårdad, ånkårdad.
from Gk. kharadrios (acc. of kharadrios 'a yellowish bird'); also in Amh. kårädayon (from Geez). See Conti Rossini, RSO 10 (1951).18-9, and 18, n. 5.

meaning: 'iron ring that holds the plowshare to the plow beam';
origin_explanation: from Amh. kårfäs.

k°arg°äne 'camp, surrounding of a camp, court, hall (of palace, church), public place, circuit, an enclosed place, enclosure, (T.B.M) assembly, retinue'; (k°arg°äne tä'ayyänt 'camp'); (k°arg°äne mangaśt 'royal palace'); see also k°ark°ana.
from Gk. khoirogryllos 'hyrax'. See also 'akrigalyon, 'agrågalyon, garågalyo, garogårliyos, garogårla.
for karägayo; see kirogårliyos.

phonetics: k°arha (yak°räḥ) (D 1432 instead of karha in D 833), (T) k°arha
meaning: 'constrain, force'; k°arḥ 'compulsion, constraint, aversion' (ba-k°arḥ 'involuntarily, by force');
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. 'akraha ('alā) 'compel', karḥ 'aversion'; related to PBh. krh (ḥif-'il) 'to Aram. karḥā 'force'. Brockelmann 1928:343 identifies it with Aram.-Syr. krh 'be ill, be ill-tempered'. The connection with Akk. karû 'be short' suggested by von Soden 452 is unlikely. The connection between G. k°arḥ and Amh. k°ärra 'be proud' (also Tna. k°är'e, Arg. kårräḥa, Gur. k°ärra) suggested by Cohen 1939:25 is doubtful.

meaning: in the meaning 'aversion', G. k°arḥ comes close to Te. kårḥa 'decline', Tna. kårḥe 'keep back'.

origin_explanation: from Ar. karåkiyy (pl. of kurkiyy 'crane'); also in Som. kurki. In the other Semitic languages: Aram. kurkāyā, Syr. kurkāyā, Md. kurkia, Akk. kurkû going back to Sumerian (von Soden 510).
could it be a misprint for kårur hčč?

meaning: ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna. ka°ka° 'almonds and nuts', Amh. kårka.

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. k°åråkk°ådä 'tie up, shackle'. The root is not to be compared with ('an)k°ark°ara 'roll', as suggested by Růžička 35. See also k°adk°ada.

also Amh. kårkåden, Amh. kårkådən, kadkådən, kadkådanä, Syr. karkadnā, Ar. karkand. Note that Krauss 299 quotes Gk. karkhēdonios 'Carthaginian' that he translates 'Karfunkelstein', that is 'Carthaginian stone'. See also karkand.
this seems to be the meaning of karkaden on the basis of the only example quoted in Dillmann 839. Dillmann ib. interprets it as wood that is red, and connects it with karkaden I.
see also k°arg°ana.
from Ar. karkand; to be identified with karkaden I. Růžička 161 compares it with Heb. kadkod 'precious stone'. On the Hittite origin, see Rabin 1964:152. See also kådkod.

ETHIOPIC: Amh. kårkånd, kårkåndä 'unicorn' (from Geez). Hommel 328, 332 considers Ar. karkaddan, karkadann 'rhinoceros' a Geez loanword. On Syr. karkadānā (and Ar. karkadann) being considered a Sanskrit (khadgin) loanword, see Brockelmann 1928:346.

meaning: 'grind coarsely'; makarkar 'that which grinds (jaw, teeth), (T) means of grinding coarsely';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. karkara 'cut in big pieces, grind'.

Tna. k*rk*r* 'grind coarsely'. Tna. k*r- k*r*, Amh. k*rk*r*, Gur. k*r*k*r* (< krkr). While Sa. k*rk*r 'grind' is taken from Semitic-Ethiopic, the root is probably common with Cushitic in view of Bil. k*r 'break'. See also makark, below.

meaning: 'roll (tr., intr), roll around, roll along, roll off, revolve, overturn'; '(T) 'pulverize, render fine' seems to refer to karkara, above. *nak*ark*r* or, *nak*ark*r* 'rotation, rolling, whirl- pool'; (*nak*)*ark*r* *ar*a naf*s* 'whirlwind'; *ank*ark*r* or (Lt) 'rolled'; *ank*ark*r* *ar*o (T) 'rolling' (n); *mank*ark*r* or, *mank*ark*r* 'turning round, wheel, chariot, circle, circuit, path';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. karkara 'turn the millstone', karra 'turn round', Ar. (Dat) karkara 'turn round', Ar. (Syria) karr 'roll, roll up', Ar. kura(t) 'ball' (see for this root Noeldeke 1910:158-9), SAr. krr 'measure of weight' (for the possibility of its being round, cp. Akk. kakk*r*u 'metal disk weighing one talent', Heb. kikk*r* (from k*rk*r) 'round loaf of bread, disk of gold or silver used as a weight', PBH. kiker 'go around', Aram. kakk*r* 'ball', Ug.Ph. kkr 'talent'. Akk. kakk*r*u 'metal disk weighing one talent, round loaf of bread'; also Aram.-Syr. k*r*k 'go round, encircle', Md. krk, for krkr. The root krkr 'dance' occurs in Heb. kirker, Ug. krkr (Aistleitner, no. 1383). ETHIOPIC: Tna. *ank*ark*r* *ar*a 'roll', *ank*ar*r*w*, k*r*k*r* 'be round', Te. k*rk*r* 'roll', *ank*ark*r*, *ank*ar*r* 'roll down', kori 'ball', m*nk*rk*r 'car- riage, wheel', Amh. (t*n*)k*r*k*r* *ar*a 'roll', *m*nk*ark*r* or 'wheel', Gur. (t*k*)k*r*k*r* *ar*a 'be lumpy (flour)'; also in Cushitic: Sa.Bil. karkar 'be round'. The root k*rk*r 'be round' is not to be connected with karkara 'grind coarsely', as suggested by Marrassigni 1971:99 and Conti 1980:85. See also karir, and k*rk*r* II.
possibly to be identified with k*k*r* I, the making thin derived from being rolled.
see q*r*q*r* III.
from Gk. kyriak*n* 'church'.
perhaps distorted from Lat. carbunculus (Müller).
see k*r*l*y*son.
see k*r*l*y*son.

meaning: 'spend the rainy season, spend the win- ter, be of the preceding year (wine)', '*akrama, causative'; kar*m* 'who spends the rainy season, one of preceding year (wine), one year old (wine)'; k(a)ramt (pl. k(a)r*m*t, k(a)r*m*t) 'rainy season, rains (June-July to Sep- tember), year'; m*kr*m* 'wintery'; m*kr*m* 'winter quarters, winter residence where one spends the rainy season' (*beta m*kr*m* 'winter residence'); makramiy* (Lt) 'winter quarters, place where one spends the rainy season' (from Amharic);
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: connected with Ar. karuma 'be > yield rain' (cp. Ar. rahma 'rain', rah- mercy on'; for dialectal rahma, see 1923:1201). Note that Dillmann's statement "karuma 'pluviam profundit (nubes)'; dein- ficeus, generosus fuit'" should read 'beneficus, generosus fuit'; deinde "pluviam profundit (nu also the three months el-karam*t (dialectal "sans doute ainsi appel*s parce qu'il pleut (Landberg 1923:1453). ETHIOPIC: Tna. kr*m*ti 'rainy season', Te. Amh. Arg. k*r*m*t, Gaf. k*r*m*t*, Har. k*r*m. Praetorius 1879:158 identifies Amh. m*s- k*r*m, name of the first month (correspo September) with m*sk*r*m 'Regen bringend' from

from Ar. karm. In the other Semitic languages: Heb. kerem 'vineyard', Aram.-Syr. karm*, Ug. krm, Akk. karr*nu 'vine, grapevine, grapes' rather than k*r*m* 'ruin, ruin heap' as suggested in Orientalia 37 (1968). 357. W. Müller 1985:272 compares Mh. karmaym 'mountain', Hars kermaym with Heb. k*r*m.
the first element is perhaps from Gk. keramos (gen. keramou) 'potter's clay, jar' (Müller).
could it be a transcription of Heb. karmel 'new corn'?
see karama.

SEMITIC kr*: Ar. kur* 'front leg, shank', SAr. kr* 'leg of an animal', Ar. (Yemen) kir*n, Soq. *er*eh*n* 'foot' (with ki > *), Heb. k*r*-*ayim* 'shank, split bone', Aram. kar* 'leg', Syr. k*r*a, Md. kraia, Akk. kuritu. ETHIOPIC: Tna. k*rn* 'elbow, forearm', Amh. k*rn* 'elbow', kand 'arm', Gaf. k*nd* (for nd:rn, see Cohen 1931:380), Arg. k*rr* 'arm, elbow', Gur. k*rr* 'arm, cubit', Har. kurru 'cubit'; also in Cushitic: Wol. kure, Mo. hiro 'arm, cubit' (h < k). For the other Cushitic languages, see Cerulli 1951:45 under hill*o*. Reinisch 1887:225 also compares k*r* 'foot of bed'.
perhaps from Lat. corpus 'body'. See also *olkorap, korep I.
perhaps from Lat. corpus (Christi); see karap.
see k*ahapa.
probably in connection with kirub.
from Gk. graphida (acc. sg. of graphis) 'stylus for writing on tablets'.
see k*araba, above, but perhaps to be connected with karapa 'cleanse through rubbing or scratching'.

meaning: (T) 'wash, cleanse'; *akrapa (T.Sw. in D 1397), causative; also 'wash, cleanse'; *akrap (T) 'pure';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Grimme 1914:261 connects it with SAr. m- krb 'Reiniger, Gereinigter (= priestlicher König)'. See also krp.
corrupt form of karasp*r*s*s, below.
ETHIOPIC: Te. k*r*qa 'rain heavily'.
see k*r*y*q*, below.

ETHIOPIC: Tna. kristiyānā 'be a Christian', Amh. kristiyānāwī 'Christian'. From Gk. khristos; also Tna.Amh. kristiyān 'Christian'. Te. kastan; Tna. kāstānā 'become a Christian'. On the church building, see Hyatt 109ff., Hammerschmidt 1967:91-2, Heyer 27ff.

meaning: 'belly, stomach, womb, abdomen, interior (of ship, ark, vessel)'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. kirs, karšī 'belly', Sh. ekreš (pl. of širs), Heb. kāreš, Aram.-Syr. karsā, Md. karsa, Akk. karšu. ETHIOPIC: Tna. karsī 'belly'. Te. kāras, kärsät, Arg. Gur. kärs, Har. kärsi, Gaf. ǝrsā (k > h zero), Amh. kärsam 'one who eats much, one who has a big stomach'.

from Ar. harīta 'bag'; also Tna.Amh. karāğīt, Har. xarīğa, Gur. kärātit; in Cushitic: Or. karātit. The form karāšit (with š) is probably hypercorrection through Amharic where s > š is normal; therefore t > š by hypercorrection.
see (karaya I), karuy.

meaning: 'leaf of a book, scroll, roll, letter, slate, parchment, paper, leaf of paper; kartasa 'write' (denominative);'
origin_explanation: from Gk. khartēs 'leaf of papyrus', through Ar. qirṭās; also Tna.Amh. kartas 'parchment, paper', Syr. kartīsa.

meaning: 'dig (a well in the ground), make holes, dig up, excavate, peck (the eyes), make cuts or incisions'

causative; also 'dig'; takaraya, passive; karāyī (pl. karayt, karāyǝyān), act. part. of karaya; karuy (fem. kǝrīt) 'dūg, excavated'; karyat 'digging, excavation, hole, pit'; makray (Gr.260) makrāy 'instrument for digging, pickaxe, spade'; makrīt (D.Gr.260) 'shovel, spade';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. karā (kry) 'dig', Heb. kārā (kry), Aram. karā (kry), Md. kra, Ph. kr, Ug. kry (Ga- quot 339). ETHIOPIC: Te. kāra (kry) 'cut off (by digging), kāre, Har. xara, Amh. kärāyäyā 'dig, till the earth'. Could Te. kāray 'hyena', Be. karāy be connected with G. karāyī 'that which digs', referring to the hyena that digs up corpses?. See also karaya II.

meaning: 'stir, excite, incite, make haughty, puff up, blow up, (Y.M) get takarya (Lt) 'become glowing, become hot';'
origin_explanation: (Note that B 75 ጽጋብ ጸጋብ näfäw sǝgäb näfäw corrected to ጽጋብ ጸጋብ sǝgäb näfäw karayo rendered by Amh. ṭǝgäb 'satieté, plenitude' is doubtful. The correct reading seems to be karayo 'the plenty pf of food (ṭǝgäb) made haughty or puffed him up (karayo)'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. kǝrrāyäyā 'become enraged', (kry) 'attack'. Or could it be a transferred meaning of karaya I 'dig > stir up, incite'? See also kǝrǝrātātā.
perhaps to be identified with karaya II 'incite', takaraya 'become gloomy'.
from Gk. kyrie eleēson, same.
corrupt from Coptic khoiahk, koiahk. See also hǝk, kǝrāq, qǝryāq'.
from Gk. kyriakē 'day of the Lord, of Last Day'. See also kǝrāq'.

meaning: 'proclaim, decree, edict, teach'; takriz 'proclamation, preaching'; karz (K*) 'proclamation, preaching'; takriz 'proclamation, edict, (K) blessing, sanctification';
origin_explanation: from Gk. kǝryssō 'I proclaim, I preach' through Chr.-Ar. karaza. On the Greek origin of this root, see Buhl 911, Koehler 1087. On the Aramaic origin, see Eilers 594.
SEMITIC: perhaps Ar. karaza 'lean toward'. For karuz, Müller suggests Lat. crux.
from Ar. Kurz 'shepherd's bag, sack'.
see kws.
from Ar. kīs (of Aramaic kīsā) origin; see Fraenkel 197. For the Akkadian (kīsu) origin of this noun, also for the origin of Heb. kīs, see Zimmern 20; also in Te.Amh.Arg. Gur. kīs, Har. kīs.
see kws.

meaning: 'circumcise'; ǝksaba, causative; takasaba, passive, reflexive; kǝsub 'circumcised'; kasbat 'circumcision'; maksab (K*) 'foreskin';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. kawāšib (pl. of kāsib) 'wild animals, birds of prey', from a root ksb 'snatch away', cp. Mh. kesōb 'snatch, disarm', Ar. (Oman) kassāb 'highwayman'; related to Heb. kāsap 'break' (Driver 1935:62-3), Ar. kasafa 'cut out (clothes), hock (a beast), Akk. kasāpu 'cut, break off a piece'. ETHIOPIC: Te. kāsba 'circumcise', Tna. kānsābā, kānsābā (with augmented n), Gur. (a)kāssābā 'pound the shell of grain'.
from Ar. kasaba; also Amh. kāšābā, Te. kāsba, Tna. kāšābā.

meaning: 'neck'; '(əgzəfə kəsädo 'he is stiff-necked, he is stubborn'); (kəsäd yəbəs 'stiff neck, stubbornness');
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Sh. kensed 'shoulder', Md. ksada 'throat' (so Drower-Macuch 224; Kaufman 64 translates it 'a neck ornament'), Akk. kišadu 'neck, throat'. Dietrich 298 considers Md. ksada an Akkadian loanword. ETHIOPIC: Tna. kəsäd 'neck', Te. səgad (with metathesis and assimilation: *səkad > səgad), Amh. kəsäd 'back of the neck'. Reinisch 1895:150 compares Be. kešiya.

meaning: 'defecate, relieve the bowels'; ḳʷšä 'causative, also 'defecate'; kʷš 'filth, excrement, dung'; (məkäna kʷšä, mudäya kʷš 'ash 'latrine'); makʷšäḥ 'latrine'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Dillmann 842 connects it with Ar. kasaha 'sweep' with a possible development 'sweep > sweepings > filth > dung'; ETHIOPIC: Te. kʷšä 'soil, desecrate', Tna. kʷšäḥi 'excrement (of chicken)', Amh. kʷšäsa 'defecate', kʷšas, kus 'excrement', Arg. kaš 'dung (of chicken)', Gur. kʷšä barä 'have dysentery'; also in Cushitic (rather than 'passed into Cushitic' as in Leslau 1979:354): Al. kus, Sid. kose, Or. koši 'dirt'.

meaning: (M 432 going back to kʷšä) 'make peace with one another' is given by M on the authority of Tayyä, but Tayyä has only the meaning of kʷšä mentioned above.

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. kʷšäšti 'phial of glass or metal', Amh. kʷšäš 'water jug'; also in Cushitic: Kham. kuskusä, Af. kuskusti.

meaning: (T.M.) Hebrew month; transcription of Heb. kisləw.
'Orion (constellation)'; transcription of Heb. kasîl; could kasilä be Aramaic with the Aramaic article -ā?

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. kʷəšmi 'saint to whom a church is dedicated', to be identified with ksm 'cut' (Heb. kəšam, Akk. kasāmu) > *separate > *assign > dedicate'. For Heb. kirsem, see Greenspahn 130.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. Amh. kasma 'tent peg'.
from Gk. kosmos, same.
from Amh. käššäšä. Grébaut, Aethiops 3 (1930), 45 renders it 'break to pieces' and derives it from Ar. kassa. However, in the sentence given by Grébaut the verb kss could also mean 'accuse'.

meaning: 'sweep, cleanse, wipe away, get rid of debris, dust (and so on), purify, prune (trees), correct'; takʷəsätära, passive; kʷəsätäri, act. part. of kʷəsätära; kʷəsätur, pass. part. of kʷəsätära; also 'dirt (that 'what is swept')'; kʷəsätär 'sweepings'; kʷəsätär (K*) 'sweepings'; makʷəsätär 'rubbish heap, sweepings, refuse (T.S.K) broom, (L.D) twigs (serving as a broom), (L) snuffers for a candle'; makʷəsätär, (Lt) makʷəsätär 'broom, snuffers'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. kʷəsätäri 'sweep, wipe off, filter', Te. kʷəsätära 'sweep away', Amh. kʷəsättära 'wipe off, sweep away', Gur. kʷəstärä 'filtered'. See also kʷəsara.

meaning: 'separate, divide, in → stroy, dissolve, decompose, belittle, disprove, refute'; takʷəsaya, passive; kʷəsyat, kʷəset, verbal noun of kʷəsaya; also 'abrogation, dissolution';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to kss, ksh, as in Ar. kassa, kaskasa 'pound, grind', Heb. kāsas 'divide into portions', kāsah 'cut off brushwood', Syr. kas 'break into', Akk. kasāsu 'chew up'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. kʷəsäya 'break into pieces', to kʷəšhe 'break into pieces', Amh. kʷəsäkʷäsä 'break a stone or a clod of earth'. For 'belittle, disparage', cp. Amh. kʷəššä (from kʷsy) 'joke', Gur. (a)kʷəšä, Har. qʷəsa; also in Cushitic: Or. qōsi, Som. qōš.

meaning: 'bear a surname, have the same name as someone else'; makʷsi, makʷsəy 'who has the same surname'; makʷsäyäyan (pl) 'homonyms';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. mäkʷsi 'si 'namesake', Te. Amh. mäkʷse, mokʷse, Gur. mokʷse; passed into Cushitic: Qem. mokʷse. See also mkʷsy.

meaning: 'be variegated, speckled'; kʷəsäkʷəsä, kʷəsäkʷəsä, kʷəsäkʷəsä, kʷəsäkʷəsä, (T) kʷəsäkʷəs, (L) kʷəsäkʷəs 'speckled, variegated, multicolored, spotted, black and white (horse, cow)'; (həbra kʷəsäkʷəs 'of variegated colors');
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. kʷəsäkʷəsä 'paint something multicolored, speckle'.

meaning: 'uncover, disclose, lay bare, expose, reveal, make manifest, discover, open (eyes, mouth, box)'; takašata, passive; also 'be visible, appear, uncover oneself'; kašati, act. part. of kašata; (kašate bärhan 'illuminator'); kašut (fem. kašatt) 'revealed, uncovered, manifest, obvious, clear, evident, public (eye), visible'; (kəšuta, ba-kəšut 'visibly, openly, obviously, plainly'); kašutawi (Gr) 'pertaining to being revealed'; kašit 'disclosure';

(kašita täšəṭa 'incarnation'); kəšät 'uncovering, disclosure, revelation, opening, (Gr) prayer composed of verses taken from the Psalms and recited at funeral services'; makšat 'opening (n), act of opening, (KG) who uncovers, who reveals';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Brockelmann 1927:28 compares it with Ar. kašafa 'reveal, disclose' > G. kšt, the r of which is due to 'Reimwortbildung' with the opposite kšr 'hide'. ETHIOPIC: Te. kästa 'open', Tna. käšätä, Amh. käšätä (from Geez).

meaning: 'write down, write, register'; takatba (Lt), passive; kətabi 'scribe'; kətab 'writing, message, letter, book, text, document, charm, amulet';
origin_explanation: from Ar. kataba 'write', kitab 'book'. Also Te. kätba 'write', Har. kätäba, Gur. kätäbä, Amh. kättäbä 'vaccinate', Tna.Amh.Gur. kətab 'charm, amulet'. Let see also katapa.

meaning: 'bind firmly, tie up'; takatfa (K), passive; kətuf 'bound firmly, tied up'; kʷətfat, kutfat, (T) kətfat 'binding (n), bond, tie';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. katafa 'tie the hands behind the back'. Soq. kətf; but also a metathesis in: PBh. kəpaṭ 'tie', Aram. kəpaṭ, Syr. kəpaṭ 'form into a knot', Md. kpt 'tie, bind', Akk. kapātu 'become compact'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. kʷətfat-ä əd 'hands bound behind the back' (from Geez). See also kʷətfat.
from Gk. katholikos; also Te.Amh. katolik, Tna. katolik, Har. kotälik.

meaning: 'seal, stamp, close, vaccinate, tattoo, incise'; (Amh. atäggärä given by Y as the translation of G. katama means 'tattoo' rather than 'be black', as rendered by M);
'be vaccinated, be tattooed'; (in the meaning 'disguise oneself' takatma is the transcription of Ar. taḥattama, according to Guidi 1899:458);
origin_explanation: (from Ar. ḥatama 'seal'; also Te. (ˀa)kātäma 'make an end', katmät 'end', Amh. (ˀa)kātämä 'be finished, come to an end', Tna. kātämä 'be finished'; Te. kātma 'seal', Amh. kātämä 'print'; in Cushitic: Sa. kātīm 'seal' (n.). See also katama II.

ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna.Amh.Gur. kātāma 'military camp, city', Har. in kātämbäri 'gate of the com-pound' (from kātäm[a] bäri); Tna. kātämä 'estab-lish a permanent camp'. However, it is also possible that the original meaning of katama is 'compound' (as in Har. kātämbäri 'gate [bäri] of the compound [kātäm]) and could be derived from katama I 'seal', that is, 'close in'.
