Ludolf 402 (followed by Dillmann 852) identifies katama 'camp, military camp' with katama 'extremity, top, stating, "vulgo vocantur 'castra regina', scil. summa", that is, 'top, summit'.

meaning: 'camp, be encamped, (Y) move here and there';
'extremity, utmost, tip, top, summit; camp, military camp';

origin_explanation: from Ar. kattān 'linen' (for its Aramaic origin, see Fraenkel 42; for its Persian origin, see Vollers 1896:647); also Amh. koṭān 'linen'. Som. kiṭān. In the other Semitic languages: Heb. kuṭṭōnet 'tunic', Aram. kittānā, Syr. kettīnā, Md. kitana 'linen', Ug. Ph. ktn, Akk. kiti, kitintu (considered to be of Sumerian origin). For Gk. khitōn 'undergarment, coat' borrowed from Semitic, see Masson 29. For more details on this root, see Baumgartner 480-1.
see kataba.
ETHIOPIC: perhaps Amh. kaṭābe 'kind of tree'.

meaning: 'curtain off, screen from view, bar, prohibit, forbid access, hinder, stop, abstain, detain, determine a boundary, (K*) make an enclosure, surround';
origin_explanation: (katara bäḥra 'build a dam'); (bä'ala katara 'eve of Epiphany', cel. the 10th of Torr; so called because of the practice of damming a stream to form a pond); katārīt, fem. of katārī (T*) 'lion's den', lit. 'that which bars, encloses'; SEMITIC: connected with the root ktr 'surround, make a hedge', a meaning that developed into 'prohibit access'. Heb. kitter 'surround', Md. kṭir 'detain, retain'. ETHIOPIC: Tna.Gur. kaṭāra 'prohibit, prevent access', Har. xātāra, Tna. kātāra 'make a fence-in place', Te. kātära 'surround, a hedge', Amh. kātāra 'screen, surround a fence, dam a flowing stream'; passed into Qem. kaṭār 'dam up, embank'.

meaning: 'capital of a pillar, (Gr) crown, wreath of flowers';
origin_explanation: transcription of Gk. khōtar, khōthareth, kotere! 'capital (of pillar)' also Amh. k°ätār 'capital of a column' (from Geez).

SEMITIC: Ar. (Dat) kaṭṭ 'demolish, cut down', Heb. kaṭṭ 'crush fine, crush to pieces', Aram. kaṭṭet 'pound, crush', Ug. kṭṭ 'beaten (copper)'. Ak. kaṭṭa 'be short' (from a meaning 'be cut down'). ETHIOPIC: Te. kaṭṭa 'hurt, beat', Tna. kaṭṭa 'cut', Amh. kaṭṭa 'cut in little pieces, chop up (wood)', Gur. kaṭṭa 'break into pieces, 'break bread in half' (from kṭ?), k°äčče 'hashed meat' (from kṭy).

'askataṭa (Lt) 'mobilize' (from Amharic). ETHIOPIC: Te. kaṭṭa (kaṭṭ) 'call together', ˀaṭaṭa 'gather', Tna. kaṭṭa 'gather, be mobilized', Amh. Gur. kaṭṭa 'gather, mobilize troops', Har. kaṭṭa 'store', also in Cushitic: Sa. Af. kaṭ 'gather, come together'.
from Gk. khettīm, a wrong transcription of Heb. bäyit resulting from a misreading of ב י ת.
from Ar. hiṭmiyy.
from Gk. khytrogawlos 'kind of pot'.
see qaṭārāqten, and kaṭārāqāṭen.

ˀakwafa 'collect (liquid that falls into a receptacle), (L) draw forth, (T) cause a puddle to form'; SEMITIC: Ar. (ta)kawwafa 'assemble (people)' that Dillmann 866 suggests reading yāwakkafa 'accept, they receive' (see wkf) instead of yākawwafu. ETHIOPIC: Tna. (ˀa)kofa 'collect the urine of cows'.

takawwala 'stay behind, remain behind, follow behind, be the rearguard'; kawwal (K*) 'who remains behind'; kawala, kawala, k°äla, (Gr.465) k°äwla 'hind, hind part, back part, rearguard'; kawala, kawala (adv) 'in the rear, afterwards, later on, in the back'; SEMITIC: related to Ar. (ta)kayyala 'keep on the rear' (Dillmann 860). ETHIOPIC: Te. k°awal 'rearguard', käwwala 'hide', Amh. h°äla 'behind', Gur. f°äla, with k° > *f (Praetorius 1879:522; Leslau 1979:231); also käwwala 'hide, protect' from an original meaning 'stand behind to protect'.
see *kolala, ankōlala.
SEMITIC: perhaps Ar. kāma 'cover (an animal)' [Am-bros].

meaning: 'be, become, there is, there was, exist, happen, occur, come into being, come to pass, take place, be done, be permissible', with object suffix pronouns 'be sufficient (for), be enough (for), behoove (to)'. Note that the predicate noun or adjective with kona 'become' is used in the accusative; e.g., kona naguśa 'he became king'.
origin_explanation: kona + imperfect expresses habitual action in the past; kona + perfect expresses the pluperfect; zakona, zakona kawino 'whoever, whatsoever, whatever happens, whosoever he may be'; takawana (K*) 'be finished'; ˀakona (D 1432) 'cause to be'; kon 'sufficiency'; the attested example is that of the 1st pers. sg., that is, konaya 'it is suf-ficient for me'; or would it be from kona with a not-attested suffix pronoun of the 1st person?; kawani, act. part. of kona; kawaniṭ, kawaniṭ (pl. kawānāyaṭ, [KG] kenotāṭ) 'creature, being, image, column, obelisk; that which is a place of rest (that is, that which serves for something)'; kawwan 'established'; kawin 'being (n), nature'; kawina 'condition, quality'; kawwāne 'being, origin, generating, essence, existence, nature, statute, condition, quality, manner'; kunaṭ, k°ənaṭ 'being (n), coming about, origin, essence, mode, situation, station'; makān (pl. makānāt) 'place, space, room, occa-sion'; (makāna 'in the place [of])'; makānāṭ (pl. of makān) 'oratory'; SEMITIC: Ar. kāna 'be, exist', SAr. kwn 'be, occur', Mh. kān 'be', Heb. kwn (hif'il) 'be firmly estab-lished', Aram. ken 'so, thus', Syr. kān 'be, exist',

phonetics: Md. kan, Ph.Ug. Kn 'be', Akk. kānu
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Tna.Gur. konā 'be', Te. vi.kon 'no', Amh.Gaf. honā (with k > h), Arg. honā, Har. xāna; also in Cushitic: Bil. kun 'be', Sa. kān.

meaning: 'priestly garment', undoubtedly confusion with kawānim (see above), the form kawānim being corrupt from kawām.
= Amh. agodo 'pit in which the blacksmith melts iron'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. kūr 'blacksmith's furnace', Heb. kūr, Aram.-Syr. kūrā. Zimmern 32 derives it, with reser-vations, from Akk. kūru (not so von Soden 512; Kaufman 65). There is also a form kūr (Heb.Ar) considered an Akkadian loanword according to von Soden 484, but not according to Kaufman 65. ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh. kāwr 'furnace, melting pot'; Cushitic: Af. kūr borrowed from Arabic.

meaning: 'steer a ship', takawwara 'steer a ship, keep a steady helm, (T) turn, go back and forth'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. (South Arabia) kawwara 'place a boat in the water'. ETHIOPIC: 'turn', cp. Amh. (tān)kāwarrārā 'wan-der while searching'.

meaning: 'agitate, stir, incite, shake, mingle, mix'; akosa, takosa, (T) akwasa 'move (tr), con-found, trouble, perturb, disturb'; takawsa, takawsa, passive; also 'move (intr), be turned away, blaze'; takāwasa 'be perturbed, be moved (tossed) here and there'. kawwus, pass. part. of kawasa;

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. (ak)kawāsā 'stir up', (from a root kss or from ks with reduplication of the last radical), kus bela 'move', Amh. kāwwāsā 'shake, stir'. Reinisch 1895:149 compares kwās 'stir'. The root is perhaps to be identified with kws II.

meaning: for kosa 'rate, sweep, destroy'; see also kws I.
'mix', denominative from *kawsat, from kawasa I). See also kosata.
'hasten, hurry, be quick, be swift, be agile, be smooth (words), lie, cheat'. kawāw 'rapid, energetic, swift, agile, (words)'; kəwāw (K) 'rapidity, speed, agility'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. kāwwa 'wandering'.

meaning: with nominal suffix pronouns expresses the personal pronoun as direct ob-ject; it is used for special emphasis, e.g. kiyā-ya 'me', kiyā-hu 'him, it, the very same'. It is followed by a noun in the accusative: e.g. kiyā hā mādra 'even it, the land', that is, 'the land itself'. At times it takes the place of a demon-strative pronoun; e.g. ba-kiyā-hom mawā'əl 'in those days', kiyā-hā lelita 'on that night'. The direct object may also be expressed with the object suffix pronouns attached to the verb and with kiyā-; e.g., kiyāka tašāhalakka 'he was gracious to you' (possibly with emphasis).
origin_explanation: the derivation of kiyā from k*əlayāt (pl. of k*əlit) 'kidneys' > 'self' suggested by Praetorius, ZDMG 27 (1873), 640 is not convincing. Note that for a noun with suffix pronouns used as a pronoun he quotes Tna. nəssu-u 'he' from nāfsu 'his soul', or Amh. əssu, əssu 'he' from rā'su 'his head'. It would seem that kiyā- comes from *kə-a, to be compared with zīv-a- from zə- (see also Rundgren 1955:188). As for kə it is perhaps a prepositional element (cp. Amh. kā) which becomes in Geez an element for the expression of the direct object.

meaning: 'tread, tread down, trample, trample down, thresh (by treading), akeda, same; also 'make tread, make trample'. takayəda, passive; takayəda 'tread one upon another, trample'; kayəd, act. part. of keda; kayyəd 'trodden down'; kayyəd (Lt) 'trampling, treading'; kayyəd 'something trodden down'; kidat 'tread down, stepping, footstep'; makayəd, makayəd (pl. makayəd) 'footstool, stool, step, platform, footprint, base (of a column or of an altar), sole of foot, foot (as measure), shoe'. məkyād (pl. məkyādāt) 'tramping-ground, trampled ground, threshold, threshing floor, winepress, oil press, footprint, foot (as meas-ure)'. (məkyāda wayn 'winepress'); (məkyāda 'əkl 'threshing floor');
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to Ar. kadda 'press hard', kadīd 'ground trampled upon' (Dillmann 872). In connection with Heb. kīdōn 'dart' suggested by Buhl 343 and Koehler 433 is unlikely. ETHIOPIC: Te. keda 'tread upon'. Tna. kādā 'walk', Amh.Gur. hedā (with k > h) 'walk, go', Arg. hedā, Har. xēğa 'stamp, trample'; passed into Cushitic: Sa. kayad 'tread upon'.

meaning: 'take testimony (of wit-ness)'. takayəda 'stipulate, demand a promise, enter into a confederacy, make a treaty, form an alliance, make a pact, make a covenant, be bound by a covenant, promise, pledge'; (takayəda kidāna 'make a treaty'); 'astakayəda, causative of takayəda; also 'unite by covenant'; kidān 'covenant, pact, treaty, testimony, alli-ance, will, testament, promise made by God to the Virgin'; (kidān balit wahaddəs 'Old and New Testa-ment'); (kidāna mahrat 'testament [pact] of grace' [epithet of Mary]), promise made to a saint to grant all the prayers performed in his name'; (mas(a)hafa kidān 'The Book of the Covenant' containing the teachings of Jesus during the 40 days before the resurrection); for a bibliogra-phy, see Hammerschmidt-Six 1983:62.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. kidān 'pact, covenant', Amh. (tā)kayyādā 'enter into a pact with one another', kidān 'pact, treaty'.
could it be for heda, above, with k > h? W. Müller suggests Ar. kāda (kyd) 'outwit, deceive, plot'.

meaning: the fourth Coptic month (corresponding to G. tāhsās); from Coptic khoiahk, kiahk; also Chr.-Ar. kiyahk. See also kiyārəq*, k*əryāq* I, qəryāq*.
'be sharp, be clever'; a denominative from kin, created by KWK.
the fourth Coptic month; see kiyāhak.

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. kāys 'snake', Tna. kāyəs, kāysi. Amh. kāysi (from Geez).

could it be a corrupt form of kā'əse (see above) 'contents of the stomach' and possibly also 'stom-ach', as in Te. kā'əs 'stomach'? For 'stomach' and 'liver', cp. G. kābd 'liver' and Amh. hod (< kābd) 'stomach'.
W. Müller suggests an Arabic origin: hizā, pl. of hizwa, huzwa 'arrow'; however, Ar. h becoming k in loanwords is not normal.

meaning: (MA) = Amh. dällašä 'repair something broken by plastering over the cracks, anoint the hair, anoint the hair with butter and plaster down the hair with it'.

origin_explanation: metathesis of the root zkr, zakara, tazkir 'remembrance'.
